experiments.title: How a donor goal on a high urgency campaign pushdown impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor goal to the February Sustainers Pushdown for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the potential donor would feel like their monthly gift would have a larger impact on the goal.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/03/2025
During The Navigators February Sustainers Campaign, we decided to test into adding a donor goal into the pushdown copy.
The first treatment variation set a blanket donor goal of 100 monthly donors, and the second treatment variation set donor goals for each amount depicted on the gift array (i.e. Will you be one of the 50 monthly donors to make a $15 monthly gift, 30 donors to make a $25 monthly gift, or 20 donors to make a $50 monthly gift today?)
The goal of this test was to determine whether The Navigators’ audience would respond positively to a defined donor goal, and if so, whether they were more motivated by an overarching goal or one broken up into different amounts that felt more attainable.
Our hypothesis was that breaking down the donor goal into smaller, specific amounts would make it feel more achievable, increasing the likelihood of giving. By seeing a clearer, tangible impact, donors would be more inclined to contribute, believing their gift could make a greater difference.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor goal to the February Sustainers Pushdown for homepage visitors significantly increased donor conversion metrics. The treatment variation that set donor goals for each amount depicted on the gift array resulted in a 266.8% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control group, with a 96.8% statistical level of confidence.
It is possible that Treatment #2 caused more confusion and friction on the page rather than the very clear goal of 100 donors in Treatment #1. This suggests that The Navigators audience reacts positively to clarity and set goal amounts.
Furthermore, Treatment #2 had a 301% lift in revenue at a valid 97.4% level of confidence. Not only did the overall donor goal motivate more people to give, but it motivated them to give more than the control.
In future experimentation, we should continue to incorporate donor goals into our fundraising campaigns. By setting specific and attainable donor goals, donors are more likely to feel that their contribution will make a difference and be more motivated to give. Additionally, breaking down the overall donor goal into smaller amounts can make the goal seem more achievable and encourage more donors to participate.
Overall, the success of this experiment demonstrates the importance of setting donor goals and providing donors with a clear understanding of how their contributions can make an impact. This strategy can be utilized in future fundraising efforts to drive donor engagement and increase donations for The Navigators.

experiments.title: How including a donation ask earlier in an email appeal affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a donation ask upfront will increase donor conversion rate because readers will see the call to action before they potentially lose interest or skim past it, making it more likely that they take action immediately rather than waiting until the end of the email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/26/2025
This experiment was conducted on Email 5 of The Navigators’ Sustainers Campaign, which focused on acquiring new recurring donors.
The goal was to determine whether placing the donation ask at the beginning of the email would increase donor conversion rates.
Given that email recipients often skim content or lose interest before reaching the end, we wanted to test whether an upfront call to action would drive more immediate engagement and donations.
This experiment was motivated by the need to optimize email structure for maximum impact, ensuring that potential donors saw and acted on the ask before disengaging.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

1. Placing the donation ask at the beginning of the email resulted in a significant decrease in donation conversion rates compared to placing it at the end.

2. The hypothesis that including the donation ask upfront would increase donor conversion rates was proven to be incorrect in this case.

3. The control version, with the donation ask at the end of the email, outperformed the treatment in terms of donation conversions.

Recommendations for Future Experimentation:

1. Consider the flow and structure of the email carefully. While it may seem intuitive to place the donation ask upfront for immediate action, this may not always yield the best results.

2. Test different placement strategies for the donation ask in future email campaigns to see which location generates the highest conversion rates.

3. Continue to track and analyze data from A/B experiments to inform decision-making and optimize future campaigns for better results.

experiments.title: How beginning a high urgency appeal email with a story impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that beginning an email appeal with a story for CYE Email 4 recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because the reader will be immediately engaged and drawn in by the narrative therefore increasing the reader retention.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
For Email 4 of The Navigators CYE campaign, we opted to test into removing a personal reflection from the beginning of an appeal email.
We believe that starting the email with the story directly will immediately engage the reader by drawing them into a compelling narrative, leading to increased reader retention and higher click-through rates.
This approach bypasses the personal reflection, which may delay the email’s emotional hook, ensuring the audience connects more quickly with the mission and impact of the Navigator staff.
The treatment was sent to 50% of the Email 4 recipients.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

1. Beginning an email appeal with a story for CYE Email 4 recipients resulted in a 44.0% increase in clicks compared to starting with a personal reflection.

2. The treatment showed a high level of statistical confidence at 97.4%, indicating that the results are reliable and significant.

3. By immediately engaging the reader with a compelling narrative, the treatment increased reader retention and click-through rates.

Future Experimentation:

1. Consider incorporating storytelling at the beginning of email appeals for other campaigns to enhance reader engagement and drive desired actions.

2. Test different types of stories, such as success stories, impact stories, or personal narratives, to determine which resonates best with the audience.

3. Continue to prioritize testing and data analysis to identify effective strategies for engaging donors and supporters in email communication.

By leveraging the success of this experiment and applying the key learnings to future campaigns, The Navigators can continue to optimize their email appeals for increased impact and engagement.

experiments.title: How including a gift array in a high urgency campaign sticky bar affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a gift array on a sticky bar for website visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/17/2024
The Navigators are running a sticky bar on their website as a part of their calendar year-end campaign. They wanted to test how including a gift array on the sticky bar would affect donor conversion rates.
They believed that more people would choose to give once getting to the donation page because the user has already begun the process of giving by selecting how much to give.
This test ran for 1 week and the treatment was shown to 50% of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a gift array on a sticky bar can significantly increase donor conversion rates. In this particular case, the treatment group experienced a 292.8% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control group, with a statistical confidence level of 100.0%.
This suggests that providing website visitors with the option to select a gift amount before they even reach the donation page can streamline the giving process and encourage more individuals to make a donation. Moving forward, we should consider implementing gift arrays or similar features in future experiments and campaigns to further test and optimize their effectiveness in driving donor conversions.
Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of making the donation process as easy and convenient as possible for users, ultimately leading to increased engagement and support for The Navigators' mission.

experiments.title: How a personal ask to match a gift amount affects overall conversions in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a personal ask to match a gift for donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because  it creates a sense of urgency and personal connection, motivating donors to feel their contribution has a direct and meaningful impact, especially when paired with the opportunity to double their support through matching.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/04/2024
The Navigators wanted to test how a more personal approach to asking to match a gift on Giving Tuesday would affect overall conversion rates including clickthrough rate from the email to donation page and donation conversion rate.
They tested this concept within the four emails they sent on Giving Tuesday. The control group received the same four email and the treatment group received the same treatment emails.
The control emails presented the matching gift by stating a generous donor has offered to match all gifts up the gift amount like below –
This is vital and exciting work! And we have a generous donor who agrees and who has committed $100,000 as a matching challenge to encourage others of like heart to show their generosity today on Giving Tuesday.
Whereas the treatment emails presented the matching gift by stating there was a generous donor who wanted to personally match their gift like below –
This is vital and exciting work! And we have a generous donor who would like to encourage you to give personally, [First name] through a matching challenge.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that a personal ask to match a gift for donors significantly increases the clickthrough rate from the email to the donation page. The treatment group, which received the personalized matching gift ask, saw a 57.0% increase in clicks compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 100.0% confidence level, demonstrating that the treatment was more effective in driving donor engagement.
Additionally, when comparing clicks from the email to donations on the donation page, we saw a 30.6% decrease in donor conversion rate with a 99% level of confidence. But we believe these numbers to be skewed since the treatment sent so much more traffic to the donation page. Therefore we calculated the email delivered to donation conversion rate and saw a 9% increase in donor conversion rate with a 67.7% level of confidence.
Although those results did not validate, we believe this test to have an overall positive impact as it drove so much more traffic to the donation page.
In future experimentation, The Navigators should consider implementing personalized asks for matching gifts in their communications to donors. This approach creates a sense of urgency and personal connection, motivating donors to feel their contribution has a direct and meaningful impact. By offering donors the opportunity to double their support through matching, organizations can potentially increase donor engagement and achieve higher conversion rates. It is important to continue testing and optimizing personalized approaches to matching gift asks to further enhance donor response and maximize fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How more hyperlinks within a high-urgency campaign email appeal impact email clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a higher number of links  for Eagle Lake email appeal recipients will achieve an increase in email clicks because it gives the reader more opportunities to navigate to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/08/2024
Every year, The Navigators run a high-urgency campaign called Eagle Lake 2 between June and July to raise money for the camps they host.
We have seen success using more hyperlinks within an email appeals in terms of both unique clicks and transactions, therefore, we wanted to try testing this for The Navigators.
The control maintained the number of clicks that we used in each of the previous appeal emails – typically 1, occasionally 2. However, the treatment contained a total of 5 links to the donation page.
Our hypothesis was that giving the reader more opportunities to navigate to the donation page the more likely they are to click.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing the number of links in email appeals for The Navigators' Eagle Lake campaign led to a significant increase in clicks from recipients. The treatment group, which had 5 links to the donation page, saw a 32.8% increase in clicks compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a confidence level of 97.5%. These results are valid - meaning it is recommended to use this tactic moving forward if your goal is to increase email clicks.
Further, we also wanted to see what the downstream metrics looked like on the donation page for this test. From email clicks to donations, the treatment saw a 10.5% decrease in transactions at a 40.4% level of confidence. While from email sends to donations, the treatment actually saw an 18.9% lift in transactions at a 52.4% level of confidence.
It is important to view both of these metrics as the different perspectives offer very different results. While these metrics did not validate, it is important to remember that when we push more traffic to a donation page, the instant conversion rate with decrease. But in actuality, there was an 18.9% lift in transactions.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of the number of links in email appeals. This suggests that providing recipients with more opportunities to navigate to the donation page can result in higher engagement and potentially more donations. It would be interesting to test different variations of the number of links to determine the optimal amount for maximizing clicks and conversions.
Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of testing and optimizing email strategies to enhance the effectiveness of fundraising campaigns for The Navigators.

experiments.title: How a relevant image in a high urgency email appeal impacts email clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a relevant image for Eagle Lake 2 email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because people give to people and it would establish a more personable connection to the camp.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/08/2024
Every year, The Navigators run a high-urgency campaign called Eagle Lake 2 between June and July to raise money for the camps they host.
The Navigators audience is very comfortable and familiar with Abby Anderson, the main sender we use for their emails and texts. Therefore, we wanted to see whether an image of Abby at Eagle Lake camps when she was young would influence email clicks and increase donations as a result.
Our hypothesis was that the image of Abby would allow the recipient to feel more connected to Eagle Lake camps therefore increasing the likelihood that they would click the donation link, then give a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a relevant image of Abby at Eagle Lake camps in the email led to a significant increase in clicks for all traffic. The treatment group, which received the email with the image, saw a 48.1% increase in clicks compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 99.9% confidence level, indicating a strong impact of the image on recipient engagement.
These results are valid and therefore, we should consider using relevant images within appeal emails moving forward.
We also pulled number of donations out of the number of clicks within this test and saw an invalid 25.7 decrease in donations at an 85.9% level of confidence. As well as number of donations put of the number of email sends resulted in a 10.1% lift in donations at a 33.4% level of confidence.
We decided to view both of these metrics because based if we only viewed the number of donations out of clickthrough rate, it appears that the image of Abby decreased conversions, but in actuality, there was a 10% increase in donations. It only seems as though there was a drop because we pushed a larger group of people to the donation page.
Neither of these metrics validated, but the results do offer helpful learnings and context of The Navigators audience.
This experiment demonstrates the effectiveness of using personal, relatable images to establish a more personable connection with the audience and drive engagement. In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue incorporating relevant images of people or scenarios that resonate with the audience to increase interaction and response rates. Additionally, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of testing different elements in email campaigns to identify strategies that yield the best results and improve overall campaign performance.

experiments.title: How a donation interrupter impacts recurring giving during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the recurring interrupter for Eagle Lake Donation Page donors will achieve an increase in recurring giving because the donor is asked to give less upfront but have a larger impact.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/09/2024 - 05/17/2024
Every year, The Navigators run two high-urgency campaigns focused on raising money for their Christ-centered summer camp called Eagle Lake. We have been actively testing ways to activate their donor file to monthly sustainers, and we wanted to bring these efforts into their Eagle Lake Campaign.
This interrupter only showed to half of the people who selected a one-time gift under $100 on one of their Eagle Lake donation pages.
The copy asked the visitor to convert their gift to a $10 monthly gift in support of Eagle Lake efforts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment show that the addition of the interrupter may have had a positive impact on converting The Navigators audience to become monthly sustainers.
A total of 120 donors were shown the interrupter and out of those 8 donors clicked "Yes" to convert their gift to a $10 monthly gift.
That is 6.67% conversion rate!
While we are unable to validate these results, it is encouraging to see almost 7% of donors willing to commit to a monthly gift and become a Sustainer.
We plan to continue testing the presence of the interrupter in an effort to create a larger Sustainers file for The Navigators.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different approaches to increase recurring giving, such as optimizing the messaging and design of the interrupter, as well as testing different donation amounts and frequencies. Additionally, it is beneficial to analyze the donor file to identify specific segments that are more likely to convert to monthly sustainers and tailor the interrupter messaging accordingly. By continuing to test and optimize these strategies, nonprofits like The Navigators can effectively increase their recurring giving and generate more sustainable revenue for their programs such as the Eagle Lake summer camp.

experiments.title: How adding clarity and impact to the homepage slide impacts user engagement and donor conversion metrics

rows.name: Treatment - Praying the Names and Attibutes

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that utilizing a new content offer for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in visitor engagement on the homepage as well as donor conversion metrics because the additional clarity and impact of the content offer will garner more attention from visitors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2024 - 07/17/2024
The Navigators homepage gets a lot of organic traffic. Therefore, we wanted to use this as leverage for the acquisition of new emails and donors to the organization.
The control homepage offered little to no detail about what resource was being offered in the header image. So we wanted to test how adding clarity around what the offer is and the impact the offer will have in an individual’s life would affect click-through rate and other conversion metrics.
Additionally, we wanted to unlock which offer The Navigators audience would engage with best.
We ran an A/B/C split test with the variates based on content offers:
Version A – Control – Discipleship 101
Version B – Praying the Names & Attributes of God eBook
Version C – How to Memorize Scripture eBook
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B/C test conducted on The Navigators' homepage with three different content offers - Discipleship 101, Praying the Names & Attributes of God, and How to Memorize Scripture - resulted in an impactful outcome.

Treatment 1, which featured the content offer "Praying the Names & Attributes of God," showed a significant increase in visitor engagement and donor conversion metrics. Specifically, there was a 124.8% increase in Hero CTA button clicks for All Traffic at a 99.9% level of confidence. As well as a 254.1% lift in Hero Button Clicks on Treatment 2 which featured "How to Memorize Scripture: with a level of confidence of 99.9%. 

These results indicate that both treatment versions outperformed the control in terms of Hero Button Clicks 99.9% of the time. 

We also wanted to see what the downstream revenue looked like for these offers. We used new appeal codes for this test so that we could accurately pull these results.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to a landing page headline and copy impacted sign-up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity to the headline and the copy for site visitors will achieve a higher sign-up rate because it will better communicate the value they will receive by signing up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2023 - 11/10/2023
In an effort to increase our sign-up rate on the landing page of this Christmas Cards offer, we wanted to test adding clarity to both the headline and the copy. In Variation 1, we tested the new headline by itself. In Variation 2, we tested the new headline and the new call to action above the sign-up form.
The new headline reads:  Get your free scripture-infused Christmas Cards
The additional copy reads: Simply complete this form to request your free Christmas cards
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding clarity to the headline and call to action button on the landing page resulted in a decrease in email acquisition, compared to the control group. Variation 1, only testing the headline, resulted in a 2.4 drop with 19.5% level of confidence. Variation 2, testing the new headline and the statement above the form, resulted in a 31.2 drop in email acquisition with a 99.9% level of confidence.
On the surface, this suggests that the changes made did not resonate with site visitors and may have made the offer less appealing which is why we saw the drop in email acquisition.
It is important to note that this landing page experienced issues with this form after this test was launched. As well as, this experiment only ran for four days which could have heavily impacted our results. Therefore, we plan to retest including clearer language on landing pages, in hopes that we will see an increase in emails acquired.

experiments.title: How a sticky slideout on content pages affects overall donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional opportunity to give for site visitors  will achieve an increase in donations because they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2023 - 11/15/2023
The Navigators wanted to take advantage of the traffic generated through their resource and blog pages. As people are being equipped/informed, we wanted to run a test where people would see a sticky donate button on the side that would prompt people to give. The treatment would slide out when someone scrolled halfway through the page. If they click “yes” they would be taken to a donation page to make their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. The addition of a sticky donate button on the side of the resource and blog pages resulted in a significant increase in donations. The treatment group experienced a 36.5% increase in transactions compared to the control group, with a 99.7% level of confidence. This suggests that the prompt to give, along with the value proposition language, was effective in persuading visitors to make a donation.
2. The Sticky Donate Click rate increased by 10,3203% with a 100% level of confidence. This indicated that the presence of the sticky donate button garnered more attention and engagement from site visitors.
3. The Sticky Donate-Yes Button Click rate saw a 17% increase with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that visitors were receptive to the call-to-action and were willing to proceed to the donation page.
4. Surprisingly, the Sticky Donate-No Button Click rate experienced a significant increase of 74,8344% with a 100% level of confidence. This may indicate that even if visitors chose not to donate, they were more engaged and actively responding to the prompt.
Based on these findings, it is evident that the addition of a sticky donate button can be an effective strategy to increase donations. The value proposition language used in the prompt seems to have resonated with site visitors and motivated them to support The Navigators.

experiments.title: How yes/no “autonomy” language on a pop-up impacts conversion rate during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a yes and no option for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitor to feel in control before being asked for the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2024 - 04/27/2024
For The Navigators April Eagle Lake High Urgency campaign, we wanted to test using two buttons, both a yes and no option, on their homepage overlay/pop-up.
We hypothesized that providing the visitor with a micro-decision would lead to an increase in the donor conversion rate.
The control features the standard single call-to-action. Whereas, the treatment offers the call-to-action to donate as well as a “No, I’m not interested option.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that providing visitors with a choice between a yes and no option on the homepage overlay significantly increased the donor conversion rate for The Navigators April Eagle Lake High Urgency campaign. The treatment, which featured both options, resulted in a 193.8% increase in donations compared to the control, with a statistical level of confidence of 97.2%. This demonstrates that giving visitors the autonomy to make a micro-decision before being asked for a donation can positively impact conversion rates.
In future experimentation, The Navigators should continue to test and implement strategies that offer visitors a choice or a sense of control in the donation process. This can include using two buttons with different options, adjusting language to frame the call-to-action as a choice rather than a directive, or incorporating interactive elements that engage visitors in decision-making. By leveraging the learnings from this experiment, The Navigators can optimize their donation process and ultimately drive higher conversion rates and engagement with their campaigns.

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity of a subject line affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the clarity of the subject line for email respondents will achieve an increase in open rate because the appeal extends to a larger audience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/14/2023
The Navigators were sending out a cultivation email as the initial email for their Sustainers campaign. The control email used the subject line “What group is on your heart today?” The team hypothesized that this subject line assumed that people understood what the organization meant by “group”, and that the reader did in fact have a group on their heart.
They proposed a treatment, “Who are you praying for today?” that would increase the clarity and appeal to a larger group of people.
experiments.keyLearnings:
From this experiment, we have learned that a more clear and relatable subject line in an email can lead to an increase in open rate and ultimately, in this case, a higher rate of donations.

The key learning from this experiment is that using language that appeals to a wider audience and is easily understood can be more effective in engaging recipients and motivating them to take action. The treatment subject line, "Who are you praying for?" was able to resonate with a larger group of people compared to the control subject line, "What group is on your heart today?". This likely contributed to the increased open rate and subsequent increase in donations.

In future experimentation, we should prioritize using clear and relatable language in our subject lines to capture the attention of recipients and encourage them to open the email. Additionally, we should continue to test different variations of subject lines to optimize our messaging and maximize engagement and conversion rates.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of crafting subject lines that are clear, relatable, and appealing to a wide audience. By continuing to test and refine our messaging strategies, we can optimize our email campaigns and drive greater impact for our nonprofit organization.

experiments.title: How do the perceived benefits of a premium affect clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that explaining the benefits of a premium book for potential donors will achieve an increase in clicks because  the additional clarity on the premium will motivate them to click the link and learn more.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
For The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to test calling out the perceived benefits of the premium in an effort to pinpoint motivating factors for their audience. The control email called out the impact that their monthly gift would have on the organization whereas the treatment called out the benefits of reading the book that they would receive as a premium for becoming a monthly donor.
This test ran as a 50/50 split to their housefile audience in an effort to acquire more recurring donors as well as test what motivates their audience to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that explaining the benefits of a premium book for potential donors led to a significant increase in clicks compared to highlighting the impact of a monthly gift. The treatment saw a 27.8% lift in clicks at a 98.8% level of confidence, indicating that these results are valid. We can conclude that providing additional clarity and tangible benefits to potential donors can be a motivating factor for them to take action, and we should continue to utilize this motivation in future appeals. This test also suggests that using impact language may not be effective for their audience.
In future experimentation, we should continue to emphasize the benefits and values of becoming a monthly partner, such as offering premium gifts or exclusive content. This approach has shown to be effective in increasing engagement and click-through rates. Additionally, we should consider testing different types of premiums or incentives to see which resonates the most with our audience. By continually testing and optimizing our messaging and offerings, we can better understand what motivates donors and improve the overall effectiveness of our campaigns.

experiments.summary:

The Navigators were running direct mail acquisition and wanted to test the impact of adding brand ads alongside these pieces so that people would see ads 10 days before and 10 days after the direct mail piece arrived in their mailbox.
In the ads, they addressed that the user would be receiving Christmas cards and a calendar from The Navigators in the mailbox soon. The ads had no other call to action or button to click through to another page.

This demonstrates the importance of building brand awareness and familiarity with potential donors before making the direct ask for donations. By priming prospects with information about the upcoming direct mail pieces, the organization was able to improve the effectiveness of their fundraising efforts.
Moving forward, The Navigators should continue to integrate digital advertising with their direct mail campaigns to maximize their impact. They can use similar strategies in future experimentation by testing different messaging, creative, and timing to further optimize their results. Additionally, they should consider expanding this approach to other marketing channels to create a cohesive and multi-touchpoint donor acquisition strategy.

experiments.title: How do the perceived benefits associated with becoming a recurring donor impact donor conversion rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the benefits of becoming a monthly partner for the donor will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the perceived exclusivity and incentives associated with becoming a monthly partner are more valuable to the user.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/01/2024
For The Navigator’s February Sustainers Appeal, we hypothesized that calling out the benefits of becoming a monthly partner would increase our click-through rate as well as our donor conversion rate. The control email emphasized the importance and need for monthly partners, focusing heavily on the difference it would make for the organization. Whereas, the treatment focused on the benefits for the donor themselves.
This test ran as a 50/50 split to their housefile audience in an effort to acquire more recurring donors as well as test what motivates their audience to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted for The Navigator's February Sustainers Appeal tested the impact of emphasizing the benefits of becoming a monthly partner for the donor, as opposed to focusing on the organization's need for monthly partners.

The treatment email, which highlighted the benefits for the donor, showed a 161.3% increase in donations for All Traffic compared to the control email with a 99.6% level of confidence. These results did validate based on the 95% level of confidence that we strive for. Therefore, we can conclude that the perceived benefits of becoming a monthly donor were very effective at motivating individuals to take the next step and initiate a recurring gift. This also suggests that the incentives will work well for The Navigators audience.

In future experimentation, it would be interesting to test where we can apply this concept to other campaigns and initiatives of the Navigators, including other appeals or their Eagle Lake campaign.

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity of a subject line affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the clarity of the subject line for email respondents will achieve an increase in open rate because the appeal extends to a larger audience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/14/2023
The Navigators were sending out a cultivation email as the initial email for their Sustainers campaign. The control email used the subject line “What group is on your heart today?” The team hypothesized that this subject line assumed that people understood what the organization meant by “group”, and that the reader did in fact have a group on their heart.
They proposed a treatment, “Who are you praying for today?” that would increase the clarity and appeal to a larger group of people.
experiments.keyLearnings:
From this experiment, we have learned that a more clear and relatable subject line in an email can lead to an increase in open rate and ultimately, in this case, a higher rate of donations.

The key learning from this experiment is that using language that appeals to a wider audience and is easily understood can be more effective in engaging recipients and motivating them to take action. The treatment subject line, "Who are you praying for?" was able to resonate with a larger group of people compared to the control subject line, "What group is on your heart today?". This likely contributed to the increased open rate and subsequent increase in donations.

In future experimentation, we should prioritize using clear and relatable language in our subject lines to capture the attention of recipients and encourage them to open the email. Additionally, we should continue to test different variations of subject lines to optimize our messaging and maximize engagement and conversion rates.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of crafting subject lines that are clear, relatable, and appealing to a wide audience. By continuing to test and refine our messaging strategies, we can optimize our email campaigns and drive greater impact for our nonprofit organization.

experiments.title: How an exit intent pop up affects email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a popup offering a free ebook for users exiting a page that advertises a for-purchase item will achieve an increased acquisition of email because we believe that targeting the same motivation (scripture memory) at a free price point will acquire those who were exiting because of the price.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/21/2023 - 03/30/2023
The Navigators’ Topical Memory System is a for-purchase book that helps the reader memorize scripture. The landing page for this offer briefly outlines what the TMS is, important points, and an ask at the end to purchase the book. We hypothesized that by adding an exit intent pop-up on the page for a free eBook on a similar topic (How to Memorize Scripture) would not interfere with those who are purchasing the TMS book, but would acquire the emails of those who were planning to exit the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that offering a free ebook with an exit-intent popup can significantly increase email acquisition. The treatment group saw a 147.9% increase in email acquisition for all traffic, and new visitors, organic search visitors, desktop visitors, social visitors, male visitors, and visitors aged 18-24 saw substantial increases in email acquisition with a high level of confidence.
What does this tell us about users exiting the page? The eBook popup is about the same topic as the TMS page—but where the TMS page is urging users to make a purchase, the popup entices them with a similar offer free of cost. Because this popup is only setup to appear when the user goes to exit the page, this wouldn't detract from those who were going to purchase the book. What it does accomplish is allow The Navigators to acquire the email of someone who would've exited the page and been lost.

experiments.title: How a sticky slideout on content pages affects overall donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional opportunity to give for site visitors will achieve an increase in donations because they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language. because they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language. because they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language. because they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2023 - 11/15/2023
The Navigators wanted to take advantage of the traffic generated through their resource and blog pages. As people are being equipped/informed, we wanted to run a test where people would see a sticky donate button on the side that would prompt people to give. The treatment would slide out when someone scrolled halfway through the page. If they click “yes” they would be taken to a donation page to make their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The addition of a sticky donate button on the side of the resource and blog pages resulted in a significant increase in donations. The treatment group experienced a 36.5% increase in transactions compared to the control group, with a 99.7% level of confidence. This suggests that the prompt to give, along with the value proposition language, was effective in persuading visitors to make a donation.

2. The Sticky Donate Click rate increased by 10,3203% with a 100% level of confidence. This indicated that the presence of the sticky donate button garnered more attention and engagement from site visitors.

3. The Sticky Donate-Yes Button Click rate saw a 17% increase with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that visitors were receptive to the call-to-action and were willing to proceed to the donation page.

4. Surprisingly, the Sticky Donate-No Button Click rate experienced a significant increase of 74,8344% with a 100% level of confidence. This may indicate that even if visitors chose not to donate, they were more engaged and actively responding to the prompt.

Based on these findings, it is evident that the addition of a sticky donate button can be an effective strategy to increase donations. The value proposition language used in the prompt seems to have resonated with site visitors and motivated them to support The Navigators.

experiments.title: How moving the problem to the front of the email affects donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting the problem earlier in the email for email recipients will achieve an increase in conversion rate because more readers will understand the problem to which they can be the solution.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/14/2023
The initial email of the Navigators fall Sustainers campaign used the example of how their team was able to go from “zero to one” to get missionaries on a specific college campus in Austin. However, this fact was buried in the email, which started with mission language that was somewhat organizational-centric.
The team hypothesized that the fact that a large college had ZERO missionaries on campus would be compelling and would engage more people if it was moved to the beginning of the email.
They believed by confronting the reader with this fact (and the fact that, thanks to donors, there now ARE missionaries on campus) that they would get more donations because more readers would understand the impact of their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Positioning the problem earlier in the email was effective: By highlighting the fact that a large college had zero missionaries on campus and emphasizing the impact of donor support in solving this problem, the treatment email achieved a significant increase in donations. This suggests that readers are more likely to respond when confronted with a clear problem and understand their role in solving it.

2. Donors want to see the impact of their gift: The fact that the treatment email successfully conveyed the impact of donor support in addressing the problem suggests that donors want to know how their contributions are making a difference. Future experiments should focus on highlighting the tangible outcomes and benefits that donors can achieve through their support.

3. Consider the reader's perspective: The original email was somewhat organizational-centric, starting with mission language. However, by repositioning the problem statement, the treatment email took into account the reader's perspective and engaged them more effectively. Future experimentation should continue to prioritize the reader's perspective and focus on conveying the message in a way that resonates with them.

Based on these learnings, future experimentation should continue to explore ways to highlight the problem and its impact early in the communication. Using donor-centered language and emphasizing the tangible outcomes of their support can help increase engagement and conversion rates. Additionally, ongoing testing should be conducted to fine-tune the messaging and ensure that it aligns with the readers' motivations and interests.

experiments.title: How including a video on a slide-out impacts transactions

rows.name: Treatment - Security Box with Lock and Copy

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a slide-out featuring a video will drive more transactions than a photo. The video adds a personal and human touch, allowing prospects to see and hear directly how their donation will benefit WUNC. This engaging approach is likely to make them more inclined to contribute.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/10/2023 - 08/18/2023
This experiment, conducted for WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, aimed to determine whether a slide-out with a video or a sticky bar with a photo would drive more transactions. Both the slide-out and the sticky bar featured WUNC’s Capitol Bureau Chief, delivering a value proposition on why prospective supporters should contribute to WUNC.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the slide-out featuring a video outperformed the sticky bar with a photo in terms of driving transactions for WUNC. The video likely added a personal and human touch, making prospects more inclined to contribute. The treatment group saw a significant 107.3% increase in Transactions for All Traffic, with a statistical confidence level of 94.2%. Although this result is approaching validity, further testing would be necessary to confirm the intended effect.
Moreover, Revenue also experienced a 9% increase with a 98% level of confidence, indicating that the video slide-out had a positive impact on the financial outcome as well.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the effectiveness of different visual elements in driving conversions. The results suggest that incorporating videos with a personal touch can be a successful strategy for engaging potential supporters and ultimately increasing transactions and revenue for WUNC. It would be beneficial to conduct more experiments with varying types of multimedia content and to continue analyzing the impact on key metrics. Additionally, exploring the optimal timing, placement, and design of such visual elements could further enhance their effectiveness in driving desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How adding a relevant premium in an offer affects giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a relevant premium for offer downloaders will achieve an increase in average gift because they would be incentivized to give a larger gift when they receive something in return.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/23/2023 - 07/03/2023
Navigators has historically had mixed results with the use of premiums on an offer. We have seen the most success with premiums that are directly correlated with the digital product they’re receiving. In a new offer (an online course offer) we added a relevant premium (the book version of the ebook included) for a gift of $50 or greater. We wanted to test to see if we would get a larger average gift with a relevant premium with this particular offer. Increasing the average gift would positively impact the ROI of the offer, which had been very low.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a relevant premium for offer downloaders did not lead to an increase in average gift. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in revenue compared to the control group. This experiment resulted in a valid 75% decrease in revenue with a level of confidence of 95%.
It is important to note that the experiment did not reach the minimum sample size needed for statistical significance. We only had 9 transactions and did not meet the 50 transaction minimum.
A few things to note:
The offer in it of itself did not perform well. This offer had a high cost per name and did not get a lot of traffic which resulted in a low number of transactions before we had to turn off the experiment. In the treatment we wanted to include $50 in the gift array because there is that specific ask for the premium. Unfortunately, the starting amount was significantly lower than the control's and it's likely that this lower gift array actually resulted in a lower average gift. There was no visual way to call out the premium section. The platform limits our capability and the book on the left hand side is the only place we can add an image.
It's suggested we retest this with the following adjustments:
The same gift array for control/treatmentA visual callout of the premium language so it stands out to the reader (like a box outline around the test)
Based on these findings, it would be recommended to conduct a larger experiment with a sufficient sample size to get a more accurate understanding of the impact of the premium.

experiments.title: How moving transactional language and fund codes from the header to the bottom of the copy impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that not leading with transactional information on a donation page for prospective donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because focusing on value proposition can reduce friction and further humanize the experience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/17/2023 - 03/12/2023
While the Navigators have implemented a complete radical redesign of their donation pages, there is one element on their donation pages that must be kept as part of the template due to platform restrictions. The “Support The Navigators Ministry” and Fund ID at the top of the donation page is needed for in-platform tracking. Being unable to hide this mandatory header, we decided to test moving the placement of this information. We hypothesized that this transactional language would not inspire more people give. We wanted to give people the opportunity to read through the entire value proposition before making their gift. So, we tested moving the transactional language and Fund ID to the bottom of the experience, beneath the gift array.
This placement was tested across all the active instant donation pages for the Navigators’ offers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a valid 25% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 95%. While this result did not align with what we believed would occur, we hypothesize that there are a few reasons that people may not have given.
The change headline actually made the "thank you" line not seem like a an actual header. Though the fund ID sounds transactional - the first sentence says "Support the Navigators" and it may be that this sentence is giving the reader more clarity as to what they're supposed to do on that page Seeing thank you at the very top with no other CTA may make the person think that this is the end of the interaction.
It is recommended that we run this test with a couple new treatments:
The thank you headline with a larger font. The thank you headline with a larger font and a transition sentence that will help the reader understand that there is more action to take on  this confirmation page.

experiments.title: How adding intentional name capture links in a high traffic page affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that linking to a landing page instead of a PDF download for users interested in Bible reading plans will allow The Navigators to take advantage of the opportunity for email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2023 - 03/30/2023
One of The Navigators’ most popular resources is its Bible reading plans. We noticed that this particular resource page had a large amount of organic traffic. The existing page setup prioritized direct PDF downloads, but this lack of forms on the page meant that The Navigators’ wasn’t collecting emails or names of those who downloaded these resources. There was an email capture form at the bottom of the page but it’s likely that once someone downloaded a PDF, they would not scroll to the bottom of the page to fill out a form.
We hypothesized that by keeping the page layout the same, but just swapping out the PDF links in favor of a landing page link to each reading plan—would allow the Navigators to keep distributing this resource while also collecting emails in the process.
Additionally, we only targeted those who were first time visitors to the site so as not to diminish ministry impact for the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that swapping out PDF downloads for a landing page link is a more effective approach to acquire email addresses from users interested in Bible reading plans. The A/B test demonstrated a 433% increase in email acquisition with a 100% level of confidence.
Moreover, the experiment also revealed that the new approach was successful across various visitor types such as mobile, desktop, organic search, male, female, and varying age ranges. The highest increase in email acquisition was seen among new visitors, organic search visitors, and female visitors.
The key metric that The Navigators were concerned about was whether this funnel process would mean that the number of those who wanted to get the resource would decrease.
When looking at the control, we had 596 get the instant PDF. In the treatment we saw 64% of those who clicked the link, followed through in filling out the form to get the resource. Meaning, most people actually took the next step to give their information in exchange for the resource. Because we targeted first time visitors, these are likely new names acquired for the organization.

experiments.title: How the use of story in an email appeal impacts click thru rate and donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that continuing the story on a donation page  for email recipients will achieve a higher click thru rate and donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/10/2023
During Giving Tuesday the Navigators wanted to use a story for their email appeal. We wanted to see if we could get more people to click thru the email if we continued the story in the donation page as opposed to providing the entire story in the email. This would accomplish two things: reduce the length of the email (which we hypothesized would cause less people to click thru to give) and give people incentive to click thru so that they can land on the donation page, read the rest of the story and give. In turn, the treatment would increase click thru rate and donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: We successfully increase click thru rate by 90% with a 100% level of confidence. However, when looking at donor conversion rate, we saw a valid 62% decrease with a level of confidence of 99%. So, while many more people were motivated to click thru, those were not the people who were motivated to give. These people could've just been interested in the story but had no intention to give a gift. It's recommended that this is retested to understand how to best use stories for the Navigators audience. It's recommended that the control is set as-is and the treatment would be split into two emails: one email that's pure cultivation with the testimonial/story (no ask), and a follow up email referencing the story and including a direct appeal. It could be that splitting up the experience in this way, captures all those who were interested in the story. It allows everyone to see the evidential and opportunity for them to give later.

Navigators was running a high urgency campaign at the end of the calendar year. In order to capture more donations from web visitors we ran a test on the CYE popup/overlay. We wanted to see if we could more people to click through and donate on a pop up that was more value prop-centric with a yes/no question. The control for this experiment used campaign-specific language and the treatment asks the site visitor a yes/no question. If they select yes, they’re taking to the donation page. While the second pop up has the site visitor align with a certain value claim, it's not direct in what we're asking the person to do: donate. Previous experiment results show us that this organization's audience responds to more directs communications.

experiments.title: How re-directing traffic from a blog to a lead capture page affected email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that redirecting traffic from the blog to bible study resource landing pages for Searchers on Google will achieve growth in discipleship.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/21/2022
Originally, we were driving as much free traffic we could to maximize the free ad grant dollars to mission aligned pages. We then wanted to test if shifting our goals from blog traffic to capturing emails using landing pages.
Our goal is to offer transformational content for the purpose of resourcing and equipping those interested in discipleship with free resources.
Before, we would direct them to our blog.
After, we direct them to free resource landing pages.
Bible Study Resources is the subset of the traffic we focused on for this test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
A note on the Google Ad Grant:
While we had assumed this outcome (an increase in email acquisition) would be the case, it was still a worthy test as there are obstacles to work through with the Google Ad grant quality score filter that could've limited reach to the lead capture pages.
We aimed to overcome this filter with a better user experience which is indicated in a few metrics: a better conversion rate, time on page, and how many additional pages they visited. In this case, we achieved this with a better conversion rate which improved our quality scores and met the quality score threshold for the ad grant.
In this experiment,
We found that the blog page didn't serve our new goal well: Offering transformational content (via email) for the purpose of resourcing and equipping those interested in discipleship with free resources.
The blog converted at 1.7% CVR compared to the lead capture pages which did a better job of converting at 8.7% CVR (Treatment 1) and 6.5% CVR (Treatment 2).
The final benefit to seeing the conversion rate increase with the bible study resources is that we're able to convert this free traffic into an asset: a re-addressable audience via email at a later time.

experiments.title: How personalization, reciprocity, and communal language affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personalizing the headline, inserting communal language, and leveraging reciprocity for eBook downloaders will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/06/2021 - 01/31/2021
The Navigators were promoting a free eBook about Scripture memory. While the offer was doing well in acquisition, they felt that the donation page conversion rate could be increased through optimization. They had seen experiments where personalizing the page (since the visitor had just given them personal information) might increase conversion. To this end, they added “Thank you, FirstName” to the headline. Then, they looked at the value proposition presented to the visitor after accepting the free eBook. The language on the control donation page was somewhat abstract—after promising to deliver the eBook, it went into an explanation of disciplemaking, and then made a very transactional ask to the donor: “A gift of $50 means that 2,000 people can see this eBook.”
They hypothesized that communal language, rather than transactional language, might increase conversion. First, they changed the first paragraph to thank the visitor for their desire to memorize more scripture. This was designed to reinforce the visitor’s decision, and to build the relationship. Then, instead of just asking the visitor to “get this into the hands of more Christians”, they reminded the visitor that they had just received a free book and asked them to “pay it forward” with a donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment donation page produced a 46.6% lift in conversion—which shows that these optimizations added up to have a significant impact on conversion.

Of note—the average gift for the treatment was lower than the control, which may have been an effect of removing the $50 gift handle. This is a great opportunity for a future test: how can that be included or suggested without clouding the value proposition?

Additionally, it was observed that the donation button says "submit" and this could be tested to increase motivation at the final point of conversion.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 48.3% increase in donations with a 95% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 59.8% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 76.5% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 454.4% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How presenting the donor with a problem to solve affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting the donor with a problem for page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/14/2020 - 12/12/2020
The Navigators were sending traffic to a content offer called “Soul Care”. After signing up to receive the content offer, users were directed to a donation page where they were given the opportunity to donate and allow others to receive the offer they had just downloaded. Initially, donor conversion rates were very low. However, the team wanted to test a redesign of the page.
First, they hypothesized that the large image at the top of the page was distracting from the message, so they removed it. Then, they made several copy changes throughout the test, but one change was the primary lever that they expected to have the most impact.
The control page lacked any sort of problem for the donor to solve. It made it clear that the offer was free to the user, and that the ministry was donor-funded, but then actually made the content seem really popular—which might actually detract from the motivation to give. Finally, the CTA was vague. “Practical transferrable, and transformational” help was unclear and not inspiring (they hypothesized) enough to convince people to give.
On the treatment, they presented a problem in the third bullet: “that there are thousands of people who are faithful believers but are not fulfilling the Great Commission…” Then, after this problem statement, the donor was presented with a specific way they could help—a suggested gift of $50 would distribute the resource to 1,000 people. Finally, the offer was supported by asking them to prayerfully consider a gift—removing the burden of “give now”, and reinforcing that their gift will solve the previously stated problem.
They launched a test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 103.2% increase in conversion rate. While this experiment did not have more than 50 transactions, the split was large enough and unanimous enough across multiple devices, user segments, and channels to be statistically valid.
This shows the impact a problem, a clearly worded solution, and reinforcement of the donor's impact at the moment of giving will make on donor conversion.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 147.8% increase with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to an instant donation page impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a physical copy of the ebook for donors who give $50 or more will achieve an increase in average gift .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/04/2022 - 09/04/2022
Navigators is a ministry organization that runs a variety of eBook offers. We hypothesized that offering a physical version of an already popular offer as a premium on the donation page would increase the average gift amount. This hypothesis stemmed from two ideas: 1.) providing a physical copy is seen as having greater value than the free digital version, and 2.) a premium is a bridge of appreciation to the user. You’ve generously given this, and we want to thank you for something in return. There was $50 gift minimum requirement to get this premium.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the experiment for 30 days and saw a 48.9% decrease in conversion rate with a 99.5% confidence level based upon 75 total conversions. The data surrounding premiums for a donation amount often varies. When offering the premium prior to the actual donation, one theory is that it places the donor in the mindset of exchange. When you attach an item (in this case a book) to a specific number it could cause the donor to shift from the decision-making process of generosity to a more typical evaluation of purchase: is the item worth this?
Consider that the book they just received for free, is now having a "price" of $50 attached to it, this could cause the donor to see less value in what the organization is offering. They've been taken from the mindset of giving to one of consumption, and as a result, the donor is no longer interested.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign on a donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that guiding to prospective donor through their donation process through a radical redesign for donation page visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2022 - 08/15/2022
The Navigators recently moved donation page platforms where the previous donation page template was adjusted. The new template had the gift array at the very top of the page. Our hypothesis is that having the gift array at the bottom of the page gives the prospective donor to read supporting copy which makes them more likely to give. In addition, we made the gift array form a tabbed form (just like their previous template). Our hypothesis is that their previous template would generate more donations. The previous template guides the donor by presenting them a reason to give and making the one-time and recurring giving options easier to identify through the tabbed form. We also removed the navigation at the top of the page as part of this radical redesign.
We ran this experiment on a direct appeal premium offer running on Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a 212% lift in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 96% - making this a valid increase. This tells us that the changes made to this instant donation page make a better experience for the prospective donor: they're able to read why  they should give before taking action, they are given a clearer selection of a one time vs monthly gift, and they are giving a clearer call to action by removing the external links in the navigation bar.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that pushing up the name acquisition for to the top of the page for prospective ebook downloaders will achieve a higher email acquisition rate.

Timeframe: 07/29/2022 - 08/07/2022
The Navigators was running an offer on Discipleship 101/Spiritual Mentorship. In order to increase the email acquisition rate on the landing page, we wanted to redesign the landing page so that it closely resembled the landing page of another Navigators’ resource. We wanted to see if this new template would result in more names. This new template would push the name acquisition form to the top of the landing page in hopes to get the end user to act quicker in filling out the form.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 16% decrease in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 99%. These results indicate that the user actually needs to read through the value proposition copy laid out on the landing page before deciding to fill out the form. The new template did not allow for sufficient enough copy at the beginning of their experience to explain the value of the resource they were about to receive.

experiments.title: How reducing the giving experience from 3 steps to 2 steps impacts donor conversion rate (pt. 2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the giving experiences from 3 steps to steps for prospective donors from an ebook offer will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/20/2022 - 08/06/2022
Since transitioning to a new giving platform, The Navigators’ giving process has been split up into multiple steps and pages. When we run an ebook offer on Facebook, people now have the following experience: they fill out the form to get the ebook, get a confirmation page with the donation value prop language, click through to donate, go to a generic donation page to select their gift amount, and then a checkout process. Our hypothesis is that all these steps are causing friction for the potential donor. As much as possible, we wanted to consolidate the steps by running a redirect experiment on one of their ebook offers.
We had already run this experiment with a previous offer but we did not get a large enough sample size to validate the results. So, we re-ran this experiment with their most popular offer in order to achieve statistical validity in our results.
The treatment for this experiment reduced the process down to 2 steps so that the value proposition language was on the same page as the gift array. We also made this new page our ideal donation page by creating a tabbed form and moving the gift array beneath the copy. This is to mimic what the donation pages used to look like for this organization.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results for this experiment produced a valid 97% lift in donor conversion rate, with a level of confidence of 99%. These results indicate that the new giving experience is indeed causing friction for prospective donors. Reducing the steps down to 2 steps made it easier for the user to make their donation. In addition to that, the adjusted donation page format introduced other variables that may have also contributed to the significant lift in donations. Ideally, we would like to test adding the actual payment information and process on the same page so that we can further consolidate the steps and reduce the percentage of those who abandon the process.

experiments.title: How using direct and specific language in an email appeal impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and specificity to an email appeal  for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/16/2022
Navigators was running a high urgency campaign for Eagle Lake. They had slow traction for the first half of the campaign so we wanted to test using more direct, specific language in their email appeal. The previous emails had passive language that encouraged the prospective donor to “support the next generation.” Instead, we wanted to tell them specifically to send a kid to camp. For additional specificity, we added how many students still needed scholarships and how much it cost to send one child to camp. Our hypothesis is that this will give the prospective donor a clearer, more tangible goal and in turn would generate more donations.
As an additional note: this email was #6 in the campaign – 1 week removed from the final deadline and right before the urgency-focused emails were sent.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 218% lift in donor conversion rate with a 98% level of confidence. These results indicate that the Navigators' audience responds more to a direct specific ask and goal. Being direct on what results from their gift "sending a child to camp" vs a macro goal of "impacting the next generation" will inspire more gifts.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to an instant donation page impacts average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium to an instant donation page for ebook downloaders will achieve a higher average gift .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/14/2022 - 05/07/2022
The Navigators have an offer running on Facebook for the How to Memorize Scripture  ebook. This has been one of their more popular ebooks on Facebook. We had a lot of traffic coming through these ads an a high sign up rate. In order to increase ROI for this offer, we wanted to increase the average gift that is given on the instant donation page. In order to do so, we wanted to run an experiment where we would use a thematically relevant resource (Topical Memory System) as a premium for a gift of $50 or more.
Traffic to this funnel is primarily driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for a little over two weeks we saw a 183.6% valid lift in revenue with a level of confidence of 99.7%. When page visitors were given a specific ask of $50 or more and an incentive in the form of a premium, the average gift was $47. When there was no specific ask amount or premium incentive, the average gift was $15. We can conclude that The Navigators' audiences are motivated by premium offers and are more likely to give a larger gift when presented with a specific amount and product premium. There was virtually no difference in donor conversion rate so this informs us that the premium is not why they give but rather an incentive to give an even larger gift than perhaps initially intended.
The next step in testing for The Navigators is to consolidate the giving process in 1-2 steps instead of the current 3-step approach.

experiments.title: How an offer-focused image in an ad affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing an offer-focused image for prospective audiences will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/13/2021
The Navigators were promoting their Digital Discipleship Journey offer to a wide variety of familiar and unfamiliar audiences. The image used in the original ad was meant to display the brand value of connection through discipleship, and featured groups of people together. The team wondered if it could increase email acquisition rate on the Digital Discipleship Journey offer by showing the user an image that related to what they were going to receive, rather than people.
This was the only change they made to the ads, though they loaded several “offer-focused” images into Facebook to give it creative options.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The offer-focused ads produced a 211.2% increase in conversion rate—partially fueled by a 136% increase in clickthrough rate.
This large increase from a small change underscores the prospect's need to understand the offer in a short period of time, and how much clarity can be missing when the offer isn't reflected in the image.
Future testing will focus on taking these learnings—both from the image and ad copy—and installing them on the landing page to test congruency.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/26/2025 - 03/12/2025
The Navigators are running a pop-up on their website as a part of their February Sustainer campaign. They wanted to test how including a gift array on the pop up would affect donor conversion rates.
They believed that more people would choose to give once getting to the donation page because the user has already begun the process of giving by selecting how much to give.
This test ran for the entirety of the campaign and was shown to 50% of the traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a gift array on the pop-up for homepage visitors led to a significant increase in donations. The treatment group saw a 65.9% higher donation rate compared to the control group. Despite the positive outcome, the statistical confidence level of 81.9% is below the standard threshold of 95%, indicating that the results may not be entirely reliable.

Furthermore, the treatment saw a 104.1% lift in revenue at a 67.7% LOC. While neither of these metrics validated, they do suggest that The Navigators audience responded positively to this addition.

It is also important to note that the gift array buttons preselected the donation page with the amount selected on the overlay. Therefore, taking one step out of the process once the user landed on the donation page which may have been perceived as less work and friction.

Moving forward, it is essential to continue testing the effectiveness of the gift array on donation conversion rates, ensuring a robust and reliable methodology to draw conclusive insights. It would be beneficial to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes and different variations of the gift array to validate the initial findings. Additionally, considering factors such as donor preferences and user experience could help optimize the gift array strategy for better results in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How a goal thermometer on a high-urgency donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the presence of the revenue goal thermometer  for The Navigators CYE Donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it signals to visitors that others are also donating therefore boosting trust on the page. because  it signals to visitors that others are also donating therefore boosting trust on the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
To improve donor conversion rates during The Navigators’ year-end campaign, we conducted a test by adding a goal thermometer to the donation page.
The experiment ran from December 27 to December 31, 2025, with 50% of site traffic exposed to the treatment.
Our hypothesis was that the thermometer would positively influence donor conversion by serving as social proof, signaling that others are contributing and encouraging more people to donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the addition of a goal thermometer to the donation page did not have the intended effect of increasing donor conversion rates for The Navigators' year-end campaign. In fact, there was a 5.1% decrease in donations for all traffic during the experiment period.
