Despite these results, the statistical level of confidence was only 81.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for making valid conclusions. As a result, these results should not be considered conclusive or used for future decision making but they do suggest that The Navigator's audience had a neutral reaction to the goal thermometer.
Moving forward, it is important to understand that not all experiments will yield the expected results, and it is essential to carefully evaluate the data and statistical significance before drawing conclusions. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests with different variations or factors to determine what truly influences donor conversion rates for The Navigators. Additionally, considering factors such as timing, audience segments, and messaging may also provide valuable insights for optimizing donation campaigns in the future.

experiments.title: How including specific goals and stats in an email affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including stats and goals for 2025 will increase click-through rate because specific and measurable outcomes demonstrate the tangible impact of a donor’s gift, making the appeal more compelling and actionable.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2024
This experiment involved testing email copy for The Navigators’ donor appeals in their calendar year-end campaign. The test was conducted within an email campaign sent to existing and prospective donors, comparing two variations: the Control copy, which used general language about the impact of donations, and the Treatment copy, which included specific statistics and measurable goals for 2025.
This experiment was prompted by the desire to understand whether more detailed and concrete information about The Navigators’ progress and goals would resonate more effectively with donors. The idea stemmed from the assumption that presenting measurable outcomes and future plans would create a stronger emotional and logical connection, leading to higher engagement and click-through rates. Additionally, recent donor feedback highlighted a preference for knowing the direct impact of their contributions, which further inspired the inclusion of these stats and goals in the Treatment copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including specific statistics and measurable goals for the future in donor appeals can significantly increase the click-through rate compared to using general language about the impact of donations. The Treatment copy, which contained detailed information about the organization's progress and goals for 2025, outperformed the Control copy with a 30.4% increase in Clicks for All Traffic with an 87% level of confidence.

Additionally, we saw the treatment group increase donor conversion rate by 27% but with only a 48% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of including specific and measurable outcomes in donor appeals to further validate these findings. It would also be valuable to explore how different types of statistics and goals resonate with donors, as well as the optimal way to present this information for maximum effectiveness. Additionally, considering donor feedback and preferences when crafting email copy and appeals can help tailor messages to better meet their expectations and motivations for giving.

Overall, these results suggest that donors respond positively to concrete data and future plans, indicating that this strategy may enhance donor engagement and support for The Navigators. Further experimentation and refinement of this approach can help optimize future fundraising efforts and drive continued success in connecting with donors.

experiments.title: How including relevant testimonials on the offer landing page impacts email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including participant testimonials for Jan 1 Scripture Memory Sign-up landing page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired because sharing a positive experience from others will build more trust and confidence in the value of the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2024 - 12/30/2024
Each month The Navigators run a Scripture Memory Challenge via text. Before each campaign, we promote the next months challenge via Facebook ads, housefile emails invites, and text invites. This challenges have been very popular among their audience and their staff has had a lot of positive feedback about them. Therefore, we tested adding participant testimonials to the landing page for our December/January 2025 Challenge.
These testimonials were presented like a carousel that rotated through 7 different quotes every couple of seconds.
Our hypothesis was that adding social proof to the landing page in the form of testimonials would lead to more people signing up for this challenge.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including participant testimonials on the landing page for The Navigators' Jan 1 Scripture Memory Challenge did not result in the expected increase in email acquisition. In fact, the treatment group saw a 4.7% decrease in email acquisition compared to the control group. While the results were statistically significant at a 92.3% confidence level, they did not meet the standard threshold of 95% for validity.

This indicates that the addition of participant testimonials may not have had the intended effect of building more trust and confidence in the offer. It is important to note that further testing would be necessary to confirm these results and understand the true impact of participant testimonials on email acquisition.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the effectiveness of participant testimonials in different formats or with different messaging. Additionally, considering other factors that may influence email acquisition, such as the landing page design, call-to-action, or audience targeting, would also be valuable. By continuously testing and analyzing different variables, The Navigators can gain deeper insights into what strategies are most effective in driving email acquisition for their Scripture Memory Challenges.

experiments.title: How reordering a headline’s CTAs impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that rewording the headline to make the primary focus the campaign for October Scripture Memory landing page visitors will achieve an increase in sign ups  because making the campaign the focus relieves the privacy concerns and concerns of spam that are often associated with texts from an organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/30/2024 - 10/11/2024
Every month, The Navigators have been running a Scripture Memory Text Campaign to help their audience memorize more scriptures.
However, there has still been some confusion that this is a text campaign – a series of 7-8 texts, 1 per day. Therefore, their team decided to change the headline to read: “Sign up for Text Messages and join us for a Scripture Memory Challenge!” in an effort to emphasize participants would receive daily tests.
We hypothesized that this change would result in a lower number of sign ups due to the perceived level of commitment, privacy concerns, and spam all associated with texts.
So, we decided to test it against a new headline that focused on the Scripture Memory campaign first, rather than just signing up for texts.
Our goal was to get people bought into the campaign first then agree to receiving text messages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the headline to focus on the campaign itself rather than the text messages resulted in a slight decrease in sign-ups, indicating that visitors were not impacted, neither positively or negatively, by the headline asking them to sign up for text messages first. While these results did not validate, it is a helpful learning that The Navigators audience did not have a heightened or lessened motivation to sign up by the commitment to texts.
In the future, it may be beneficial to strike a balance between promoting the campaign and addressing potential privacy concerns. A revised headline that emphasizes the benefits of the Scripture Memory Challenge while also addressing privacy and spam concerns could potentially yield better results. Additionally, it's important to ensure that any changes made are tested with a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing them on a larger scale.

experiments.title: How positive value-focused headlines affect email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that testing positive value-focused headlines for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/01/2021
The Navigators had been trying to increase email acquisition rate on a offer they had that was doing very well in acquisition. The headline on the page offered the value proposition “Get the help you need to memorize scripture”. They hypothesized that this might imply that the visitor needed help, which might be off-putting to them.
So they decided to try to new approaches. First, they wanted to ask a question that implied that the visitor wanted to gain more knowledge: Do you want to memorize more scripture?
Then, they decided to test a headline that teased the future “superpower” of the visitor after they had absorbed the content: “Be equipped with the right scripture at the right time.”
They tested all three with the torrent of Facebook traffic that the page was receiving.
experiments.keyLearnings: Neither headline was able to validate, with more than 21,000 conversions. This was partially because the motivation from the Facebook ad was very high and the email conversion on the page was more than 35%. However, it also showed that the changes might not have been radical enough to warrant a large difference. In addition to testing upstream and downstream of this page, they wanted to next test a more radical approach.

experiments.title: How including a gift handle with the corresponding impact described in a fundraising email affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including gift handles with the corresponding impact described for potential donors to Eagle Lake will achieve more revenue because we’re providing clear, tangible examples of how different donation amounts will be used .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/22/2024
The Navigators are running their bi-annual Eagle Lake Campaign to raise money for this kids’ camp that operates every summer. In the kickoff email of this campaign, they wanted to test how including gift handles in the email would affect donor conversion rate. Each gift amount described what that amount would provide for Eagle Lake.
This experiment idea came from their direct mail piece which included “tickets” for people to purchase. Each ticket was for a different amount (same as the email) and described what each level of ticket would provide for the kids at Eagle Lake.
To keep the language consistent between the email and the donation page, the treatment donation page also had the gift handles in the body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including gift handles with the corresponding impact described for potential donors to Eagle Lake can significantly increase revenue. The treatment group saw a 40.2% increase in revenue for all traffic, which indicates that providing clear, tangible examples of how different donation amounts will be used can motivate donors to give more.
However, it's important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 82.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that while the results show a positive impact, they are not completely reliable and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the inclusion of gift handles and impact descriptions in donation appeals to see if the results can be replicated and validated. Additionally, it may be helpful to investigate other factors that could influence donor behavior and revenue generation to further optimize fundraising efforts for Eagle Lake.

experiments.title: How does focusing on an urgent need rather than a premium in the final email of a campaign impact donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of urgent language instead of an incentive for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because  the sense of urgency and a deadline will act as a motivating factor to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
For the final email of The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to test urgent language in the email vs the incentive of a premium. For the majority of this campaign, we have focused on the incentive of a book premium. However, for the final email our hypothesis was that by the final email if they have not converted to a monthly donor by this point, then the premium is not incentive enough for them to give.  Therefore, the urgent language may be motivating due to the presence of a deadline.
The control included the premium and the treatment included the urgent language.
This test ran as a 50/50 A/B test to The Navigators housefile audience in an effort to activate more recurring donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that urgent language instead of a premium may be effective in motivating The Navigators audience to give in the final hours of an appeal. The treatment email saw a 47.2% lift in transactions at an 80.8% level of confidence. While this test did not validate, the results are compelling in that, urgent language resonated well with their audience.
While the clicks did not validate for the treatment in this test, it does suggest that those who were compelled to click the link were more motivated to give once they landed on the donation page.
One thing that is important to note is the presence of the premium throughout previous emails. The lack of the premium in the treatment may have been a deterrent to give with the email because they could have received the premium in previous emails.
In future experimentation, it may be worth including the premium and have the addition of the urgent language in the treatment rather than removing the premium all together from the treatment email.

experiments.title: How does adding premium language to the donation page affect recurring donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding premium language to the donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because of the additional clarity and continuity between the language used in the email and the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/05/2024
During the Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to run a 50/50 split A/B test on the donation page. The test ran for emails 4-8 before we rolled it out for the last two emails of the campaign.
The control focused on the impact of monthly gifts and the way in which monthly sustainers are central to the organization.
The treatment was almost identical to the control other than that it also included the premium of becoming a monthly partner. This premium was included in all of the emails leading up to this. Therefore, our hypothesis was that adding the premium to the donation page would create continuity between the email and the donation page, reinforcing the incentive.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that premium language included on the donation page may have a positive impact on The Navigators audience. The treatment saw a 55.5% lift in recurring donations at an 86.3% level of confidence. While this donation page was a recurring only donation page, it is possible due to privacy concerns and certain browsers that VWO blocked a portion of their audience from the test because we did have some one-time donations also coming through.
We did see a drop in revenue at 38.6% with a 62.9% level of confidence. However, due to the fact that this was a campaign targeted at activating more recurring donors, this drop was anticipated.
Although these results did not validate, the results, in terms of number of recurring donors, were headed in a positive direction. Therefore, we chose to rollout the treatment to the entire audience for the last two emails of the campaign.
While the findings of this experiment are promising in indicating a potential increase in donor conversion by adding premium language to the donation page, further testing with a larger sample size or different variations may be needed to confirm these results. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as external events or changes in donor behavior.

experiments.title: How does focusing on an urgent need rather than a premium in the final email of a campaign impact clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of urgent language instead of an incentive for potential donors will achieve an increase in clicks because  the sense of urgency and a deadline will act as a motivating factor to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
For the final email of The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to test urgent language in the email vs the incentive of a premium. For the majority of this campaign, we have focused on the incentive of a book premium. However, for the final email our hypothesis was that by the final email if they have not converted to a monthly donor by this point, then the premium is not incentive enough for them to give.  Therefore, the urgent language may be motivating due to the presence of a deadline.
The control included the premium and the treatment included the urgent language.
This test ran as a 50/50 A/B test to The Navigators housefile audience in an effort to activate more recurring donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the urgent language in the final email of The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign did not result in an increase in clicks from potential donors. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group, with a drop of 13.3% in clicks at a 81.0% level of confidence. While these results did not validate, they do suggest that the urgent language was not a motivation factor for The Navigators audience to click the link, and that they potentially respond better to the presence of an incentive or premium.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the impact of urgent language and deadlines on donor behavior. It is possible that in this instance, potential donors may have already been motivated by the premium incentive in previous emails and adding urgent language only served to create confusion or negatively impact their decision-making process.
Moving forward, it may be beneficial to continue testing different incentives and messaging strategies to see what resonates best with potential donors. Additionally, ensuring that experiments are properly powered and have a high level of statistical confidence will help to draw more reliable conclusions for future decision making.

experiments.title: How yes/no “autonomy” language on a recurring ask pop-up impacts recurring conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a yes and no option for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in recurring conversion rate because  the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitor to feel in control before being asked for the gift. because  the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitor to feel in control before being asked for a gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/05/2024
For The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we hypothesized that “yes/no” autonomy language would provide the user with a micro-decision therefore increasing the donor conversion rate.
The control features the standard single call-to-action. Whereas, the treatment offers the call-to-action to donate as well as a “No, I’m not interest option.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a yes and no option for homepage visitors led to a significant increase in recurring donation conversions. The treatment group saw a 126.1% increase in donations for all traffic at an 87.8% level of confidence.
Although this experiment did not validate, the results suggest that The Navigators audience appreciates the autonomy provided by both a "yes" and "no" option, and in turn, they are more likely to give a gift.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of autonomy language to see if the trend of increased conversion rates holds true. Additionally, exploring other ways to empower visitors and make them feel in control of their actions could further enhance donation outcomes for The Navigator's audience.

experiments.title: How does adding premium language to the donation page affect donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding premium language to the donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because of the additional clarity and continuity between the language used in the email and the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/15/2024 - 02/28/2024
During the Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to run a 50/50 split A/B test on the donation page. The test ran for emails 4-8 before we rolled it out for the last two emails of the campaign.
The control focused on the impact of monthly gifts and the way in which monthly sustainers are central to the organization.
The treatment was almost identical to the control other than that it also included the premium of becoming a monthly partner. This premium was included in all of the emails leading up to this. Therefore, our hypothesis was that adding the premium to the donation page would create continuity between the email and the donation page, reinforcing the incentive.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that premium language included on the donation page may have a positive impact on The Navigators audience. The treatment saw a 35.6% lift in recurring donations at a 73.3% level of confidence, as well as a 29.3% lift at a 93.1% level of confidence for overall transactions. While this donation page was a recurring only donation page, it is possible due to privacy concerns and certain browsers that VWO blocked a portion of their audience from the test, hence one-time donations also coming through.

We did see a slight drop in revenue at a 10.8% drop at a 32.9% level of confidence. However, due to the fact that this was a campaign targeted at activating more recurring donors, this drop was anticipated.

Although these results did not validate, they were headed in a positive direction. Therefore, we chose to rollout the treatment to the entire audience for the last two emails of the campaign.

While the findings of this experiment are promising in indicating a potential increase in donor conversion by adding premium language to the donation page, further testing with a larger sample size or different variations may be needed to confirm these results. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as external events or changes in donor behavior.

experiments.title: How using an image of the content offer in a housefile email affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image to the email for potential downloaders will achieve a higher click-through rate because users will be able to see what they’re requesting before clicking out of the email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/23/2024
The Navigators sent a free content offer to their housefile list through email. The content offer was a free 12-month calendar and users had to provide their name, email, and home address on the landing page to receive a physical copy. Following the landing page, they were redirected to an instant donation page where they were asked to make a gift to support the Navigators work.
Within the email, they wanted to test how adding an image of the calendar would affect click-through rate from the email to the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image of the calendar to the email did not have a positive impact on the click-through rate to the landing page. In fact, the treatment group showed a 3.8% decrease in clicks compared to the control group with an 80.3% level of confidence.
Moving forward, it is recommended to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size and a higher statistical level of confidence to determine the true impact of adding an image to the email. Additionally, other factors such as the content of the email, the subject line, and the timing of the email should be taken into consideration in future experiments. It is also important to continue testing different variables to optimize the email and landing page conversion rates for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How does addressing someone directly and asking them a personal question affect click-through rate for a high urgency email?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that starting an email with a personal question for potential donors will achieve a higher click-through rate because it keeps the user engaged and helps them feel more relatable to the situation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/12/2023
The Navigators launched their calendar year campaign with an email that started with a direct statement about how Christians face problems more now than ever before. We decided to test how opening an email with a personalized question to draw them in would affect click-through rate to the donation page.
In previous testing, we’ve seen a more personalized tone perform better because it allows the user to relate to the problem at hand and causes them to engage with the email longer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that starting an email with a personal question for potential donors can have a positive impact on click-through rates. The treatment group, which received the personalized question at the beginning of the email, saw a 20.3% increase in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group with an 87.2% level of confidence.

Because of these results, we believe that opening an email with a personalized question rather than a direct statement will keep users engaged with the email causing them to be more likely to click the link to donate.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of personalized tones and messaging to optimize click-through rates. Additionally, it would be important to analyze other metrics such as conversions and donations to understand the full impact of the personalized approach. By continuously testing and iterating, we can refine our email strategies to effectively engage potential donors and drive meaningful action.

experiments.title: How a Security Callout Impacts Sign-Up Rate on Offer Landing Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a security callout at the bottom of the form for users on the How to Memorize Scripture landing page will achieve an increase in sign-up rate because it removes anxiety for those who might wonder if their information is secure or why The Navigators needs their email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/05/2023 - 06/10/2023
The Navigators’ landing page for How to Memorize Scripture was experiencing a slowing in sign-up rate. We hypothesized that adding a security callout at the bottom of the form will increase sign-up rate. This test was conducted on another offer, and was expanded to How to Memorize Scripture to see the result of more testing.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment led to a 12% decrease in sign-up rate with a 94% level of confidence. Although, this is technically just under the accepted threshold for validity (95%) this in conjunction with similar results from the same test on a different offer does  indicate that perhaps this learning should be observed for Navigators' audience.
Why would a callout for security cause less people to sign-up? Perhaps in an attempt to limit anxiety around giving up an email, the callout actually introduced it. If their security hadn't been a consideration before, and the test then bring attention to it — it may have led users to overthink about "Do I really want to give my email? Is this safe?"
The takeaway here is that perhaps security wasn't a big concern for Navigators' audience. An effort to improve the sign-up rate on the page, should move on to testing other factors that might be inhibiting conversion.

experiments.title: How adding clarity and specificity to an email subscription form impacts email acquisition rate (Pt.2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and specificity for site visitors  will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate  because it reduces anxiety and increase the perceived value of what they are receiving by subscribing.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2023 - 07/01/2023
Across their site, the Navigators has a footer where they prompt people to sign up for their emails. In order to capture more emails, we added value proposition language to this section. The treatment copy let’s people know why they would want to be part of their email list and what specific communications they would be receiving. Our hypothesis is that this added clarity, specificity and value language would increase email acquisition rate. We ran this test once and saw a lift in acquisition rate among returning visitors but a decrease among new visitors. The initial test had header language that encouraged people to commit to disciplemaking. Our hypothesis was that it was too large of a commitment for new site visitors to make. So we kept the spirit of the test (adding clarity) and changed the headline to be more accessible for people new to the Navigators.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a directional 30% increase in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 89%. While not a statistically valid result, it affirms our hypothesis that the initial header may have been too much of a commitment for newer site visitors. New visitors experienced a 33.2% increase in name acquisition with a level of confidence of 87.7%. Using the new header and continuing to add clarity in the CTA copy resulted in an increase in names acquired. In fact, new visitors experienced the larger impact in name acquisition.

experiments.title: How a Security Callout on a Landing Page Impacts Email Acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a security callout at bottom of email acquisition form for users on the How to Save a World landing page will achieve an increase in acquisition rate because it relieves the stress and anxiety around how their email will be used.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2023 - 06/03/2023
For Navigators’ How to Save the World landing page, we hypothesized that a simple security callout beneath the form would increase email acquisition rate. This short line of copy reinstates to the user that their information is secure and that they are only providing their email in order to receive the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a security callout at the bottom of an email acquisition form on a landing page may not have a positive impact on the email acquisition rate. The treatment group resulted in a 12.9% decrease in email acquired for all traffic with an 88.3% level of confidence, indicating that the control group was more effective. In addition, desktop visitors had a significant 730% decrease in email acquisition, suggesting that the callout may have had an adverse effect on this device.
In future experimentation, it is important to conduct further testing to confirm whether the security callout is effective or not. If it does not work, other possible strategies could include using social proof or highlighting the benefits of subscribing to the newsletter. Additionally, it is crucial to consider the impact of the callout on different devices and user segments to ensure that it is not hurting the acquisition rate. Overall, ongoing experimentation and analysis are vital for improving the effectiveness of email acquisition strategies.

experiments.title: How reducing the number of options in a gift array impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that updating the gift array to match the copy callouts for Eagle Lake supporters will achieve an increase in gifts because you eliminate the friction of multiple-choice and better highlight the $150 amount called out in the page copy.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/24/2023 - 05/12/2023
For Navigators’ Eagle Lake campaign, much of the language and copy focused on how a gift helps send kids to camp. The donation page called out the amount of $150 specifically, as what’s needed to send one child to camp this year. The Navigators hypothesized that by eliminating the number of choices in the gift array to either the $150 amount or $75 (while still keeping an option for other) would increase the average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a 4% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 29%. This means that the reduced number of options in the gift array had virtually no impact in people's motivation to give or not. The treatment version of the page, which only had options for $75 and $150, resulted in a 19.0% directional decrease in revenue with a level of confidence of 86%. Our hypothesis is that because the control version that included $25 and $300, there were people who were inclined to give the larger amount when presented the option.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to test the impact of different copy callouts and messaging on the donation page or how the gift array is presented from a design standpoint (having a preselected amount).

experiments.title: How prioritizing value proposition on an instant donation page’s header impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prioritizing value proposition in the donation page header for prospective donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because we are reducing anxiety by focusing on “fund” language and giving people a clear call to action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/03/2023 - 04/30/2023
This experiment serves as a follow up a previous experiment (https://app.winstonknows.com/e/8GVA) where we moved the transactional fund ID language from the header. We hypothesized that this transactional language would not inspire more people give. We wanted to give people the opportunity to read through the entire value proposition before making their gift. So, we tested moving the transactional language and Fund ID to the bottom of the experience, beneath the gift array.
When we initially ran the experiment we saw a valid decrease in donor conversion rate. We hypothesized that there were several factors that contributed to the decrease in donations:
The change headline actually made the “thank you” line not seem like a an actual header. 
Though the fund ID sounds transactional – the first sentence says “Support the Navigators” and it may be that this sentence is giving the reader more clarity as to what they’re supposed to do on that page 
Seeing thank you at the very top with no other CTA may make the person think that this is the end of the interaction.
So we ran a followup experiment where we ran 2 treatments.
The first treatment enlarged the “thank you” line so that it looked like an actual header. The second treatment enlarged the copy and  added a transitional sentence to let people know that this was not just a confirmation page, nor is it the end of the interaction. This transitional copy served as a replacement for the “Support the Navigators” copy and had more value prop-based language.
This was tested across all the active instant donation pages for the Navigators’ offers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that prioritizing value proposition in the donation page header for prospective donors can achieve an increase in donor conversion rate. By providing a clear call to action in the header and removing the transactional language (fund ID), the treatment resulted in a 60% directional lift in donations for all traffic, with a 92.0% statistical level of confidence.
Results indicate that the font size of the header had no real impact on conversion rate but when paired with the transitional sentence we saw a significant impact. Our initial hypothesis was correct in that it's likely that ebook downloaders believed that this donation page was actually a confirmation page with no further action to be taken. Additionally, the use of more value based language ("do you want to make resources like this available to others who want to grow in their faith") inspired more people to take action and refocuses people on the "why" of their gift.

experiments.title: How including branding and fund type in a appeal impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing branding and fund language for email recipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/07/2022
The Navigators were running a Missionary Care campaign where they typically refer to their giving fund by name “Missionary Care Fund.” We hypothesized that by removing the branding and fund type and focusing on the impact of the fund, we would see an increase in donor conversion rate. We believe that donors aren’t motivated by the specific name of a fund but by what their gift is accomplishing.
We tested this hypothesis in an email appeal in their high urgency campaign. Instead of “Missionary Care Fund” our treatment copy used verbiage like ” help missionaries.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
Results for this experiment did not validate. We saw a 55% decrease in donor conversion with a level of confidence of 83.5%. This could indicate a near directional decrease in donor conversion rate when removing the branding and fund language from an email appeal. Upon even closer examination, we noted that the dollar amount in the appeal was supposed to be consistent at $75 and $200 was accidentally left in the control email.

It can be determined that it is likely that the Navigators' audience are familiar with the Missionary Care Fund and are not dissuaded to give by mentioning by name. Additionally, it is surprising that the higher $200 ask in the email resulted in more donations and a higher average gift of $86 vs $31.

Because this experiment did not validate and we actually ended up testing 2 variables instead of the intended one, it is recommended that this concept is retested in a medium that will get more traffic and transactions, like a sticky bar.

experiments.title: How adding a monthly callout to a main donate page affects recurring gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly call out to the main donate page for prospective donors will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2022 - 07/09/2022
The Navigators had an initiative to increase the acquisition of monthly donors. At the heel of their recurring donor campaign, we wanted to implement a simple way to acquire more recurring donors outside of campaign season. When looking at their main donation page we noticed that the monthly option blended with the other options presented for giving, among “Anonymous Gift” and “In memory of.” It was our hypothesis that the monthly option was not prominent enough for the prospective user to select this option. Furthermore, there was no value proposition language that positioned monthly giving as a better option for the prospective donor. We decided to implement a monthly callout with two variables. The first treatment simply stated what their monthly gift would do – the core value proposition: “Help believers become disciplemakers with your monthly gift today.” The second treatment gave the prospective donor a goal and identified the person as a believer who could help others mature in their faith: “We’re looking for 250 believers to give monthly to help others mature in their faith. Will you be one?” Traffic to this main donate page was primarily driven organically.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for nearly a month we saw the most impact on desktop users. For the first callout we saw a nearly valid 50% lift in recurring donors with a level of confidence of 94%. In the second callout we saw a 11% increase in recurring donors with a level of confidence of 34%. When looking across all channels we did see a 40% increase in recurring donors with a level of confidence of 91%. Given that the highest and most statistically valid lift was seen from the first callout, we had decided to rollout this variant to 100% of their traffic. These results indicate that the Navigators' audience are more motivated by their core value proposition to create more disciplemakers. They did not respond nearly as much to the second, goal-oriented variant. It could be that the goal of 250 seemed too large for the end-user and could've deterred them from making their monthly contribution. We are proposing to test this same concept on the Navigator's staff pages as well.

experiments.title: How adding a personalized monthly call out to staff donation pages affects recurring donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a personalized monthly call out  for prospective staff donors will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/06/2022 - 07/15/2022
The Navigators recently ran experiment where they added a monthly call out to their main donation page. When they saw promising results through this experiment, we decided to run this same type of experiment on their staff pages. In an effort to increase monthly donations we drew attention to the monthly giving option on the donation page by adding a callout and adding a personalization. This personalization pulled in their first name in the call out so as to humanize the giving experience. Our hypothesis was that if we highlighted giving monthly as an option and we reminded the donor that they were in fact giving to a person, we would convert more monthly donors.
We tested this concept on a select group of staff members who were actively fundraising and were receiving a high amount of traffic on their pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When looking at all recurring gifts acquired through this experiment we saw a 22% decrease in recurring gifts with a level of confidence of 66.5%. However, when examining a specific segment, new visitors, we saw a nearly valid decrease of 43.9% with a level of confidence of 94.6%. These were surprising results compared to what we experienced on the main donate page.
We concluded that:
The main donate page's callout was actually much more focused on the impact of the monthly gift "making disciplemakers" as opposed to just stating they should make a monthly gift like this callout. New site visitors may not be as swayed to see a specific person's name on the callout. Adding the personalization in third person, i.e. "Support Joe", may seem to organizationally-centric and could deter potential donors.
Our recommendation is to run this test again across all staff pages, change the statement so that it once again focuses on the value and impact of monthly giving, and change the personalization so that it reads "Support me/us."

experiments.title: How removing branding and fund type in an appeal impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing branding and fund language for email recipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/07/2022
The Navigators were running a Missionary Care campaign where they typically refer to their giving fund by name “Missionary Care Fund.” We hypothesized that by removing the branding and fund type and focusing on the impact of the fund, we would see an increase in donor conversion rate. We believe that donors aren’t motivated by the specific name of a fund but by what their gift is accomplishing.
We tested this hypothesis in an email appeal in their high urgency campaign. Instead of “Missionary Care Fund” our treatment copy used verbiage like ” help missionaries.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
Results for this experiment did not validate. We saw a 55% decrease in donor conversion with a level of confidence of 83.5%. This could indicate a near directional decrease in donor conversion rate when removing the branding and fund language from an email appeal. Upon even closer examination, we noted that the dollar amount in the appeal was supposed to be consistent at $75 and $200 was accidentally left in the control email.

It can be determined that it is likely that the Navigators' audience are familiar with the Missionary Care Fund and are not dissuaded to give by mentioning by name. Additionally, it is surprising that the higher $200 ask in the email resulted in more donations and a higher average gift of $86 vs $31.

Because this experiment did not validate and we actually ended up testing 2 variables instead of the intended one, it is recommended that this concept is retested in a medium that will get more traffic and transactions, like a sticky bar.

experiments.title: How humanizing a sticky bar impacts donor conversion rate during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that humanizing a sticky bar for site visitors  will achieve a high donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2022 - 05/21/2022
The Navigators were running a high urgency campaign for Eagle Lake camps. In order to drive more traffic to the campaign’s donation page, we wanted to implement a site-wide sticky bar. Their team member, Abby Anderson, would be the sender of these emails so we wanted to user her likeness (image and signature) to humanize the sticky bar message. People would be hearing from her from both emails and the site which would create consistency, familiarity, and would would make site visitors feel like they’re hearing from an actual person. Our hypothesis is that the humanized sticky bar would result in an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a 50% directional decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 89%. Though this experiment did not validate, we have sufficient data to hypothesize that The Navigators' audience are not motivated to give by seeing a humanized sticky bar. It's also difficult to discern what percentage of site visitors were actually receiving the campaign emails. That means that particular group of people would not have been with familiar with Abby as an email sender. For future tests, it would be beneficial to retest this concept but use the picture of a student who needs to be sponsored for Eagle Lake Camps. Giving the prospective donor a visual of who they would actually be helping (or someone like this student), would humanize the content further without needing to know a specific Navigators staff member.

experiments.title: How including matching gift language in a high urgency campaign overlay impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including matching gift language in the EL2 overlay for website visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because donors will perceive their gift as having twice the impact and feel a heightened urgency to give within the limited-time match window.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/10/2025 - 07/29/2025
During the Navigators’ Eagle Lake 2 campaign, we ran an A/B test to evaluate the impact of matching gift language on donor conversion.
Specifically, we tested whether highlighting a matching opportunity in the overlay would further motivate web visitors to give.
The experiment was live for approximately three weeks, with traffic evenly split between the control (no match mention) and the treatment (match highlighted). Our goal was to determine if this added urgency and incentive would result in more gifts to support Eagle Lake.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this experiment, adding matching gift language to the Eagle Lake 2 website overlay led to an 8.3% increase in donations across all traffic. While the treatment group outperformed the control group, the statistical confidence level was only 27.8%, falling well short of the standard 95% benchmark needed to validate results.

Notably, the treatment group also saw a slight decline in revenue of 3.4%, with a confidence level of 53.6%. These low confidence levels indicate that neither the lift in donations nor the revenue change can be considered statistically significant.

The data suggests no conclusive evidence that the presence of matching gift language in the overlay had a positive or negative effect on donor behavior. As such, these results should not be used to inform future strategy or implementation.

Note: Donations over $500 were excluded from this analysis.

experiments.title: How aligning donation page copy with giving level impacts average gift during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an impact bullet to the donation page for a larger amount for April Eagle Lake donation page visitors will achieve an increase in average gift because the donation page copy calls out a higher impact bullet that is more in line with their previous giving levels.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2025 - 05/04/2025
Every spring The Navigators run an April Eagle Lake campaign. At the beginning of this campaign, we ran a gift array test based on the donor’s lifetime revenue, but didn’t quite see the results that we anticipated.
In an effort to get a valid learning, we tested adding an additional impact bullet to the donation page for the two higher segments of lifetime donors to try to boost their average gift. The idea behind the test is that adding a higher level of impact bullet would lead to larger gifts from a segment of people who are financially dedicated to The Navigators.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment group, which included adding an impact bullet at a higher level to the donation page for higher segments of lifetime donors, saw a significant increase in revenue for all traffic by 67.3% as well as a 300% increase in transactions at an 89.3% LOC. However, the average gift for the control was $150 and $63 for the treatment. This indicates that The Navigators audience was more likely to give with the higher impact bullet amount added but they were more likely to give smaller gifts.

Overall, the experiment provided valuable insights into the potential impact of emphasizing higher donation levels to significant donors. By refining the experiment design and testing different variables, we can continue to optimize our donation page and enhance donor engagement and revenue in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How impact callouts on a high urgency pop-up impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding impact callouts to the April Eagle Lake overlay for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it offers the tangible difference each donation amount can make.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/05/2025
For The Navigators’ April Eagle Lake campaign, we tested adding impact callouts to each gift array amount on the overlay. When a donor selected a gift amount, that amount was automatically pre-selected on the donation page, where the corresponding impact message was reinforced in the copy—creating a more seamless and compelling giving experience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding impact callouts to the April Eagle Lake overlay for homepage visitors resulted in a small increase of 0.4% in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only at 1.1%, which is below the standard threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

However, after further investigation, the treatment had a negative impact on revenue. The treatment saw a 35.6% decrease in revenue at a 61.8% level of confidence. While these results did not validate they do suggest that the impact callouts were negatively trending.

In future experimentation, we should ensure that we have a sufficiently large sample size to achieve a higher level of statistical significance. Additionally, we should consider refining the impact messaging in the callouts to potentially make a larger impact on donor conversion rates. It is also important to continue testing and iterating on different strategies to optimize the donation experience for our visitors.

experiments.title: How impact callouts on a high urgency sticky bar impacts click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including impact callouts on the April Eagle Lake pushdown for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in click rate and engagement with the pushdown because it offers the tangible difference each donation amount can make. because it offers the tangible difference each donation amount can make.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2025 - 05/05/2025
For The Navigators’ April Eagle Lake campaign, we tested adding impact callouts to each gift array amount on the pushdown. When a donor selected a gift amount, that amount was automatically pre-selected on the donation page, where the corresponding impact message was reinforced in the copy—creating a more seamless and compelling giving experience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including impact callouts on the April Eagle Lake pushdown for homepage visitors resulted in an 18.4% increase in Clicked CTA button for All Traffic. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence was only 53.1%, which is below the generally accepted level of 95%. These results indicate that the impact callouts did in fact get more people to engage with the pushdown.

Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical confidence level is high enough to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore different ways of including impact callouts or other strategies to improve donor conversion metrics.

experiments.title: How a gift array personalized by giving level impacts donor conversion metrics during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that raising the gift array for April Eagle Lake recipients with lifetime revenue between $100-$499 will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it better aligns with their giving capacity therefore prompting larger gift amounts.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/29/2025
For The Navigators, April Eagle Lake campaign this year, we chose to test into gift arrays that correlated to the users giving level.
Therefore, for individuals with lifetime revenue between $100-$499, we split the recipients in half and tested the following gift arrays. Our goal was that prompting the individuals with a higher gift array would lead to an increase in donor conversion metrics.
Control: $25,$75,$150,$300
Treatment: $75,$150,$300,$450
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that raising the gift array for April Eagle Lake recipients with a lifetime revenue between $100-$499 resulted in a 43.8% increase in donations for all traffic. While this indicates a positive impact on donor conversion metrics, the statistical level of confidence was only 43.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for validity. These results did not validate, but they are directional.

In terms of revenue, the treatment saw a 243.9% lift in revenue at a 92.6% level of confidence. This metric did not quote reach validity, but it does show that donors were willing to make larger gifts with a higher set gift array. Furthermore, the control had an average gift of $42.50 and the treatment had an average gift of $101.67.

In the future, it is important to ensure that experiments have a higher statistical level of confidence to draw valid conclusions, but due to the small list sizes for this campaign, we were unable to reach validity. Additionally, it may be beneficial to conduct further testing with different gift arrays or segments of donors to better understand the impact of varying gift amounts on donor conversion metrics. By conducting experiments with higher levels of confidence and testing different variables, we can gather more reliable insights to inform future fundraising strategies.

experiments.title: How a suggested gift amount on a funnel donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a suggested gift amount callout for Easter Card donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics, primarily average gift because  it sets a clear expectation in the visitor’s mind about the suggested donation amount and reinforces the value of the Easter Cards they will receive. because   it sets a clear expectation in the visitor’s mind about the suggested donation amount and reinforces the value of the Easter Cards they will receive.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2025 - 04/01/2025
The Navigators ran an Easter card offer in Facebook this spring. The cards were free for requesters but there was a donation ask after the landing page. In an effort to boost our average gift on the donation page, we tested adding a minimum amount callout to the donation page copy.
The treatment included one sentence that said ” Would you consider making a $25 gift to the work of The Navigators today?” No other changes were made to the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a suggested gift amount callout to the donation page resulted in a 55.7% increase in Transactions for All Traffic. This indicates that providing a specific donation amount suggestion can have a positive impact on donor conversion metrics.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 46.6%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. Therefore, these findings should not be used for future decision making.
While this test did not have the chance to validate, it does suggest that the callout increased both transactions, revenue and in turn, average gift. The average gift on the control was $6 and the average gift on the treatment was $17. This suggests that the callout helped visitors better understand the value of the cards before making their gift.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of suggested donation amounts on donor conversion metrics, but with a larger sample size and for a longer duration to ensure statistically significant results. Additionally, exploring different variations of the suggested amount callout and its placement on the donation page could provide further insights into its effectiveness in increasing average gift amount.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline on a funnel donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a continuation headline for April TMS Sign Up Donation Page will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the treatment allows the visitor to understand that there are more steps to take on the page therefore increasing engagement.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2025 - 03/30/2025
Each month, The Navigators have been running scripture memory campaigns via text. After the user signs up to join the next text campaign, they are asked for a gift. We tested into this in a previous scripture memory campaign, but the results did not reach validity so we decided to test again.
Control: You’re in the challenge!
Treatment: You’re in! Now, would you consider making a gift so even more believers can experience this Scripture memory challenge?
The treatment was shown to 50% of traffic of the April TMS Sign Up Donation Page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which included a continuation headline asking for a donation, resulted in a 6.1% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 25.6%, which is below the threshold of 95% required for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical significance. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test variations of the treatment to see if a stronger impact on donor conversion metrics can be achieved. It is also worth considering other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the campaign, the audience targeted, and any external factors that could have impacted donor behavior.

Overall, while the experiment showed a positive trend towards increased donor conversion metrics with the treatment, the results should not be relied upon for making future decisions due to the low statistical significance. Further testing and optimization are necessary to determine the true impact of the continuation headline on donor conversion.

experiments.title: How introducing a new email sender in the beginning of an email appeal affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that introducing a new sender at the beginning of an email appeal for email subscribers will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it will create a sense of novelty and engagement, capturing the recipient’s attention more effectively.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/26/2025
This experiment was conducted as part of The Navigators’ kickoff email for their Sustainers Campaign, which aimed to acquire new recurring donors.
The test was designed to evaluate whether introducing a new sender at the beginning of the email appeal would increase donor conversion rates. Given that email engagement plays a crucial role in fundraising success, we wanted to explore whether a fresh or recognizable sender could capture attention, build trust, and drive more donations.
The decision to run this experiment was driven by the need to optimize email performance and maximize donor acquisition at the start of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. **Introducing a new sender at the beginning of an email appeal can lead to an increase in donor conversion rates**: The treatment, which featured a new sender introducing themselves at the start of the email, resulted in a 41.8% increase in donations. This suggests that changing up the sender in email appeals can have a positive impact on donor engagement.

2. **Confidence in the results**: While the increase in donations was significant, the statistical confidence level was only 64.7%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered completely reliable or valid for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:

1. **Continue testing with different sender introductions**: The positive impact of introducing a new sender indicates that further experimentation with different sender personas, introductions, and stories could continue to be beneficial. Testing a variety of approaches can help identify the most effective ways to connect with donors and drive donations.

2. **Increase sample size for higher confidence**: To ensure more reliable results in future experiments, it may be necessary to increase the sample size. This can help boost the statistical confidence level and provide clearer insights into the effectiveness of different email strategies.

3. **Iterate and refine based on learnings**: While the results of this experiment may not be conclusive, the learnings can still be valuable in shaping future email campaigns. Take the insights gained from this experiment, such as the impact of a new sender introduction, and incorporate them into future iterations of email appeals to continue optimizing donor engagement and conversion rates.

experiments.title: How focusing on the impact of a donor’s gift affects click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on the impact of the donor’s gift rather than mentioning the total financial goal will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it emphasizes the immediate and tangible outcomes of their support, making the decision to give feel more personal and actionable.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2024
This experiment was conducted as part of The Navigators’ calendar year-end fundraising campaign. The experiment was conducted through an email campaign sent to The Navigators’ donor base. It involved A/B testing, where the donor audience was split into two groups: one receiving the Control email and the other receiving the Treatment email.
The experiment aimed to refine messaging by focusing on tangible outcomes that resonate with The Navigators’ mission-driven donor base. Highlighting direct impact was expected to feel more personal and actionable, avoiding the discouragement large financial goals can create, and ultimately improving conversion rates.
The goal was to test if shifting focus from organizational goals to donor impact would improve donor engagement and conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that focusing on the impact of the donor's gift rather than mentioning the total financial goal can result in an increase in click-through rate, as evidenced by a 19.3% increase in clicks for all traffic in the treatment group. This indicates that emphasizing immediate and tangible outcomes of support can make the decision to give feel more personal and actionable for donors. However, the statistical level of confidence is below the 85% threshold, meaning these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Additionally, the treatment group saw am invalid 16% decrease in donor conversion rate with only a 44% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing messaging that highlights the impact of donor support in a personalized and actionable way. By refining messaging to focus on tangible outcomes, we have the potential to improve donor engagement and conversion rates. However, it is essential to ensure that results reach a statistically significant level of confidence before implementing any changes based on experimentation. Additionally, exploring different ways to communicate impact and value to donors can further optimize messaging strategies for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How highlighting the impact of a gift impacts click rate in a high urgency appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting the impact of the donor’s gift for Email 5 recipients will achieve an increase in the click through rate because it allows the donor to connect their action directly to meaningful outcomes.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
For The Navigators CYE campaign, we tested into highlighting the impact of a donor’s gift within the email copy.
We tested this concept on Email 5 with 50% of email recipient’s getting the treatment version.
Our hypothesis was that doing so would connect their action (the gift) directly to meaningful outcomes, such as discipling young believers and strengthening faith communities. We thought the emotional appeal would be likely to resonate with recipients, inspiring a sense of purpose and urgency to give, compared to the practical framing of simply checking off a to-do list.
With this change, we expected to see an increase in the click through rate and as a result an increase in donor conversion metrics.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that highlighting the impact of the donor's gift in the email copy for The Navigators' CYE campaign did not result in the expected increase in click-through rate. In fact, the treatment version saw a 10.7% decrease in clicks compared to the control version at a 59.6% LOC.

It is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, as the confidence level is below 95%. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

Furthermore, we wanted to know whether this language produced more motivated clicks. The control produced 25 donations out of the 107 clicks and the treatment produced 19 donations out of the 115 clicks. Therefore, the treatment resulted in a 29.3% decrease in transactions at a 79.8% LOC. While again, these results are not valid, they do suggest that this type of language reduces the motivation to give for The Navigators audience.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to continue testing different messaging and approaches to determine what resonates best with donors. It may be worth exploring a combination of emotional and practical appeals to see what drives engagement and donations. Additionally, conducting experiments with a larger sample size and ensuring a statistically significant confidence level will provide more reliable results to inform future campaign strategies.

experiments.title: How more applicable tab titles on a donation form + an action oriented statement impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donation form tab title from recurring to monthly and implementing action-orienting statements for November Scripture Memory Sign-Up Donation Page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the word “monthly” will better resonate with their audience and the action-oriented statement prompts the visitor to take said action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/05/2024 - 11/21/2024
The Navigators run a monthly Scripture Memory Text challenge. After participants sign up to join the challenge, they are asked for a gift.
We noticed that The Navigators tend to use the word “monthly” rather than “recurring” however their donation form says “recurring.”
We thought that this may be causing unnecessary friction on the donation page, and we saw it as an opportunity to also test into action statements for the donation form tab titles.
Control: One-time Gift & Recurring Gift
V1: One-time Gift & Monthly Gift
V2: Make a One-time Gift & Make a Monthly Gift
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the donation form tab title from "recurring" to "monthly" did not have a positive impact on donor conversion metrics for November Scripture Memory Sign-Up Donation Page visitors. In fact, the treatment saw a 4.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic, with a 21.6% statistical level of confidence.

These results suggest that the original tab title of "recurring" may have resonated better with the audience, despite the preference for using the word "monthly" in other communication from The Navigators. It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the accepted threshold of 85%, meaning that these results cannot be considered statistically significant.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to carefully consider the language used in communication and donation forms, taking into account the preferences and expectations of the target audience. Additionally, it is advisable to conduct further A/B testing with a larger sample size and potentially try different variations of the tab titles to determine the most effective messaging for donor conversion.

experiments.title: How presenting a daily donor goal in a recurring campaign affects email click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a daily donor goal instead of a full campaign goal will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it creates a sense of urgency and allows donors to feel they are part of a tangible, short-term impact.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/05/2024
The Navigators ran their bi-annual sustainers campaign in October with the goal of acquiring new recurring donors. Their campaign goal was 90 new recurring donors, but we wanted to see how their file would respond to presenting a daily donor goal instead of an overall campaign goal. To do this, we replaced any instance of the campaign goal with a daily donor goal of 4 new recurring donors. This change was made in emails 3, 7, 8, and 12.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the presentation of a daily donor goal instead of a full campaign goal did not lead to an increase in conversion rate as hypothesized. The treatment group saw a 1.1% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group, with a statistical level of confidence of 15.5%.

Since the statistical level of confidence is below the standard threshold of 95%, these results cannot be considered valid or reliable for making decisions. Therefore, it is important not to draw any definitive conclusions from this experiment.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to explore other strategies for creating urgency and a sense of immediate impact for donors. Additionally, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is adequate and that the statistical analysis can provide reliable and conclusive results. Experimentation should continue to be a core part of The Navigators' strategy for optimizing their fundraising efforts, but conclusions should only be drawn from experiments with valid and statistically significant results.

experiments.title: How giving a monthly gift to a person instead of a fund impacts overall click through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that giving the option to give a monthly gift to a person rather than a fund will increase conversion rates because it creates a personal connection and fosters a sense of ongoing partnership.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/06/2024
The Navigators ran their bi-annual sustainers campaign in October with the goal of acquiring new recurring donors. Their campaign goal was 90 new recurring donors. In the last email of the campaign, they wanted to test how their file would respond to having the option to give to a person rather than a fund of the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that providing the option to give a monthly gift to a person rather than a fund may have a positive impact on click-through rates for acquiring new recurring donors. This suggests that creating a personal connection and fostering a sense of ongoing partnership could potentially increase donor engagement.

However, it is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant enough to be considered valid for future decision making. The 16.6% level of confidence falls below the 95% threshold typically used to determine the reliability of A/B test results.

In the future, it would be beneficial to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size to determine if offering the option to give to a person indeed has a significant impact on conversion rates. It is also important to consider other variables and factors that could influence donor behavior, such as messaging, design, and timing of the campaign.

Overall, while the initial findings are promising, further testing and analysis are necessary to make informed decisions about implementing this strategy in future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How presenting a daily donor goal in a recurring campaign affects recurring donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a daily donor goal instead of a full campaign goal will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it creates a sense of urgency and allows donors to feel they are part of a tangible, short-term impact.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/05/2024
The Navigators ran their bi-annual sustainers campaign in October with the goal of acquiring new recurring donors. Their campaign goal was 90 new recurring donors, but we wanted to see how their file would respond to presenting a daily donor goal instead of an overall campaign goal. To do this, we replaced any instance of the campaign goal with a daily donor goal of 4 new recurring donors. This change was made in emails 3, 7, 8, and 12.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that presenting a daily donor goal instead of a full campaign goal did not lead to the desired increase in conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a 5.8% decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was below 85% (17.8%), which means that these results cannot be considered valid or conclusive.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, it might be worth exploring other ways to create a sense of urgency and make donors feel part of a tangible impact without solely relying on presenting daily goals. This could involve testing different messaging strategies, visuals, or other forms of engagement to see what resonates best with donors and drives the desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How a one-time vs monthly ask impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a monthly gift for October Scripture Memory participants will achieve an increase in monthly gifts because the donation page will default to the monthly form and the visitor will be more likely to complete what is asked of them.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2024 - 11/01/2024
Every month, The Navigators run a Scripture Memory text campaign. At the end of the week long campaign, the participants receive an ask.
We tested this for the month of September, but the test was slightly biased in terms of value prop, so we retested it in the month of October.
Control: one-time ask in the donation page copy & one-time default on donation form
Treatment: monthly ask in the donation page copy & monthly default on donation form
The text copy was identical for both groups and they were asked to “make a gift” generally.
The donation page copy was identical except for a single statement asking for a one-time vs monthly gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that asking for a monthly gift in the donation page copy and defaulting to a monthly donation form resulted in a 58.3% increase in one-time donations for all traffic, but oddly had no impact on monthly giving.
