While this result is statistically significant at a 52.8% level of confidence, it falls below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

These results suggest that The Navigators audience preferred being asked for a monthly gift, but having the autonomy to make the decision to make a one-time gift.

Due to the invalid results and for the short nature of how long this test ran, we should not make decisions based on these results. However, it does open the door for more testing in terms of what motivates The Navigators audience to give whether that be one-time or monthly.

Overall, the results of this experiment should be viewed with caution due to the lower statistical confidence level, but they provide a valuable insight that can be used to inform future testing and optimization strategies for donation campaigns.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline on a funnel donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a continuation headline  for August Scripture Memory donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the visitor will be less likely to perceive the donation page as a confirmation page and understand there is another step to take.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/02/2024 - 08/20/2024
Every month, The Navigators have been running a monthly text campaign to memorize scripture. Each month, participants are sent new methods to memorize a new verse. These have been really successful campaigns, but we wanted to continue optimizing the funnel process to see further growth. This test ran on the donation page directly after signing up to participate in the campaign.
For their August campaign, we decided on testing a continuation headline which removes the “Thank you” out of the headline. Our hypothesis was that removing this phrase and adding a “would you consider” would increase donor conversion metrics because the donation page visitor would not perceive the donation page as a confirmation page.
Control Headline – “YOU’RE IN! Thank you for joining us for our Scripture Memory Challenge!”
Treatment Headline – “You’re in! Now, would you consider making a gift so even more believers can experience this Scripture memory challenge?”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment, which included a continuation headline on the donation page without the "Thank you" phrase, showed a positive impact on donor conversion metrics. The treatment resulted in an 11.3% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 55.2%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. As well as a 23.3% lift in overall revenue at an 86% level of confidence.
Although the results cannot be deemed statistically significant, it is still valuable to consider the potential impact of the continuation headline on donor conversion as well as the positive direction both of these metrics were heading.  In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to further test variations of the headline or explore other elements on the donation page that could enhance donor conversion rates. Additionally, increasing the sample size and running the experiment for a longer duration may help achieve a higher level of statistical confidence in the results.

experiments.title: How additional value proposition copy affects donor conversion rate on a calendar year end page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition copy for year end donation page visitors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2020 - 12/25/2020
The Navigators were running a year-end campaign and wanted to test whether additional value proposition copy on the donation page would increase conversion. While the page already had visuals, a story, and value proposition copy at the bottom, they wanted to add it to the top of the page to motivate people who might arrive at the page unconvinced to give.
They drafted copy that outlined their plan for the next year, making a case for support, and tested the two pages against each other.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, there was no significant increase or decrease on the page. Early on, there was a notable increase—but as the month went on, and motivation (and conversion rate) increased, the copy had less and less impact, as the visitors were more motivated to give regardless of what was on the page.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition copy to the main donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition copy for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/04/2020 - 12/25/2020
The Navigators historically had very little copy on their primary donation page. As they had set an initiative to generate more “where most needed” gifts (as opposed to staff gifts), they knew they might need to convince donors who arrived on the page to make a gift. However, they did not want to do this at the expense of staff giving. They developed a value proposition that would encapsulate the mission of the organization and placed it on the page. During the month of December they ran an experiment to see how the additional value proposition affected project giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Though there was a slight increase in project giving (that was not statistically valid), the added copy to the page produced across-the-board decreases in overall giving, which led them to end the experiment. This showed that the additional copy might have confused staff donors, even if it did motivate more people to make one-time gifts.

Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 21.0% decrease with a 91% level of confidence.
Desktop visitors had a 4.9% decrease in donations with a 93% level of confidence.
 	New visitors had a 7.4% decrease in donations with a 95% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 7.1% decrease in donations with an 85% level of confidence.
 	Direct Traffic visitors had an 11.8% decrease in donations with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 12.8% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 34.7% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Social visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How an outcomes-focused headline affects email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an outcome-based headline for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/22/2021
The Navigators were running a landing page for an ebook and wanted to test if outcome-focused headlines have an impact in conversion email acquisition? For this test we decided to create two different headlines and keeping the copy intact. The control group receives a headline that incites a demand, while the experimental group receives a headline that incites an outcome-focused action.
Control group headline: Get the help you need to memorize Scripture!
Treatment group headline: Commit dozens of the Bible’s most helpful Scriptures to memory
We created this experiment in Unbounce.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We did not see a significant change between these two groups. however, it is worth testing how an outcome-based headline could potentially increase email acquisition conversion rate when focussing on positive gains (the benefit of getting the ebook, knowledge) rather than a negative one (like putting in some work, or perhaps is time consuming).

Moving forward we could try the above hypothesis.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a high-urgency donation page through a slide out affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof through a slideout for Eagle Lake 2 donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because seeing that others are actively engaging and donating will create a sense of urgency and validation, encouraging more people to contribute.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2024 - 07/26/2024
Every year, The Navigators run a high-urgency campaign called Eagle Lake 2 during June and July to raise money for their Eagle Lake camps.
We have seen success running social proof-based tests for other clients, so we were anxious to run it for The Navigators.
For this test, the treatment version displayed a pop-out of a statement that read “FirstName from State just donated $GiftAmount”
All the information displayed was data pulled from donors to the campaign. It is also worth noting, that these contacts were pulled manually due to our lack of ability to install a pixel to track conversions on their donation pages.
Our hypothesis was that the pop-out would increase the amount of social proof experienced by the visitor on the page which would then lead to an increase in our donor conversion metrics by increasing a sense of urgency and need to donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding social proof in the form of a slideout for the Eagle Lake 2 donation page did not lead to an increase in donor conversion metrics. In fact, the treatment version saw a decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control version.

It is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, with a confidence level of only 34.4%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid, and it would be unwise to make any decisions based on them. However, we can assume that the addition of social proof may have a negative consequence in terms of donor acquisition metrics. Therefore, we will need to test this concept again to come to a clear conclusion.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a statistically significant result. Additionally, it might be beneficial to conduct qualitative research to understand why the  slideout did not have the desired effect. This could provide valuable insights that can be used to design more effective experiments in the future.

experiments.title: How yes/no “autonomy” language on a sticky bar impacts conversion rate during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a yes and no option for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitor to feel in control before being asked for the gift. because the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitor to feel in control before being asked for the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2024 - 04/27/2024
For The Navigators April Eagle Lake High Urgency campaign, we wanted to test using two buttons, both a yes and no option, on their homepage pushdown/sticky bar.
We hypothesized that providing the visitor with a micro-decision would lead to an increase in the donor conversion rate.
The control features the standard single call-to-action. Whereas, the treatment offers the call-to-action to donate as well as a “No, I’m not interested option.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that providing visitors with a yes and no option on the homepage pushdown/sticky bar can lead to an increase in the donor conversion rate. The treatment version, which included both options, saw a 19.5% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 23.2%, which is below the accepted threshold of 95%.

While these results are not considered valid, it does suggest that The Navigators audience responds well to having autonomy and micro-decisions in the donation process.

In future experimentation, we should continue to explore the use of two-button options on our homepage pushdown/sticky bar to gauge how it impacts donor conversion rates. It's important to conduct further tests with a larger sample size to ensure statistically significant results that can be used for future decision-making. Additionally, we should consider refining the language used in both the control and treatment versions to see how messaging impacts visitor responses. Ultimately, the key takeaway is that providing visitors with the autonomy to make a micro-decision can positively influence their willingness to donate.

experiments.title: How including a staff testimonial video within an email impacts donor acquisition metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the staff testimonial video for Eagle Lake 2 email appeals will achieve an increase in overall donor conversion metrics because “people give to people” and the video helps establish a personable connection with the donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/29/2024
For The Navigators Eagle Lake 2 campaign this year, we wanted to test including a staff testimonial video within an email appeal to see how it impacted donor conversion metrics. The Navigators audience tends to respond more positively to more genuine communication and real stories.
Therefore, for email 7 of this high urgency campaign, we included an applicable video of an Eagle Lake Counselor sharing how God was working within the camp this year. We expected to see an increase in overall donor conversion metrics.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a staff testimonial video in email appeals for The Navigators Eagle Lake 2 campaign can lead to a significant increase in donor conversion metrics. The treatment group, which received the email with the video, experienced a 44.5% increase in donations for all traffic. This suggests that the video helped establish a more personable connection with donors, leading to a higher conversion rate.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 75.0%, which falls below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results of the experiment should not be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
Further, we saw a 37.2% lift in overall revenue at a level of confidence of 78.2%. While this metric is also not considered valid, it does suggest that The Navigators audience responded positively to the inclusion of the video.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of incorporating more person to person connection within appeals, but ensuring that the sample size is sufficient to reach a higher level of statistical confidence. It may also be valuable to test different types of testimonial videos or other forms of personal and genuine communication to determine the most effective strategy for engaging donors.

experiments.title: How a recurring interrupter with a dynamic ask impacts recurring donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a smaller, recurring donation for one-time project givers will achieve an increase in recurring giving because it reduces the perceived financial burden, making it easier for donors to commit regularly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/21/2024 - 07/16/2024
In an effort to increase recurring donors for The Navigators, we originally ran a recurring interrupter on their main donation page. The interrupter only showed for individuals who initiated a one-time project gift under $100. The interrupter then asked the individual to upgrade their giving to a $10 monthly gift. Our hypothesis was that asking for a gift smaller than what was originally initiated would lead to an increase in recurring giving.
This experiment saw a 7.8% lift in recurring donations with a 35% level of confidence. Additionally, we tracked the amount of “yes” clicks on the donation interrupter and found that 4.98% of people who originally initiated a one-time gift of $100 or less converted their gift to a $10 recurring gift.
In this new experiment, we wanted to test adding a dynamic ask amount to the interrupter to capture more donors who were giving gifts larger than $100. The dynamic interrupter ran as follows:
$100>: $10 ask
$100-$199: $15 ask
$200-$299: $30 ask
$300-$399: $40 ask
$400-$500: $50 recurring ask
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the addition of a dynamic ask amount to the interrupter did not achieve an increase in recurring giving as hypothesized. In fact, there was a 2.5% decrease in Recurring Donation for All Traffic with a low statistical level of confidence of 15.6%. This means that the control group actually performed better in terms of recurring donations.
Despite the decrease in recurring donations, we saw that 3.47% of people converted their one-time gift to a monthly gift based on the number of "yes" clicks on the pop-up.
Although this conversion rate it lower than what we saw in the first experiment, it is to be expected. We hypothesize that the conversion rate is the same as experiment 1 for donors who initiated a gift lower than $100, but expect the conversion rate to be a lot lower for people who initiated a gift larger than $100.
We believe this is because people who were making a gift larger than $100 had the intention to give a significant one-time contribution rather than committing to a smaller recurring donation. This suggests that while the dynamic ask amount may not have been effective in increasing overall recurring donations, it did still manage to convert a notable percentage of one-time donors to monthly contributors, particularly among those giving smaller amounts.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the impact of a gift impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the impact of a gift by restructuring the copy for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it will help direct the reader’s attention to it and place greater emphasis on the impact of the donor rather than the organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2024 - 06/23/2024
In an effort to increase donor conversion metrics on The Navigators Crossword Puzzles Content Offer, we decided to run an A/B test split 50/50. With previous offers, we have seen a positive lift in transactions and revenue when calling out the impact of a gift. Therefore, we expected to see similar results on this test.
The treatment reworked the structure of the copy to direct the reader’s attention toward the portion of the copy that emphasized the impact of their gift with the expectation that we would see a lift in both transactions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that reworking the copy to emphasize the impact of a gift can have a positive effect on donor conversion metrics, specifically increasing transactions. In this case, the treatment saw a significant 69.0% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was 68.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

Therefore, while the results suggest that emphasizing the impact of a gift can be effective in driving donations, they cannot be considered valid for future decision-making due to the low level of statistical confidence.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a higher level of statistical confidence is achieved before making any definitive conclusions or implementing changes based on the results. It may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period to gather more data and increase confidence in the results. Additionally, we plan to further test this concept with The Navigators audience to help validate these findings for future offers.

experiments.title: How a simplified and user-focused value proposition affects sign-up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a simplified value prop about the user’s journey and desired impact for the DDJ landing page  will achieve an increase in sign-up rate because this will better communicate the value and focus on answering why they should be interested in the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/25/2023 - 08/16/2023
After a previous test on the DDJ landing page that tested into layout, The Navigators wanted to turn their attention to testing the elements themselves. In this copy test, we hypothesized framing the language around the user and simplifying the copy to their desired impact rather than the Navigators would increase sign-up rate. Rather than answering why the organization created Digital Discipleship, the treatment is focused on asking questions directly to the user and positioning the resource as the answer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifyign the value prop   did not achieve the desired increase in sign-up rate for the DDJ landing page. The treatment actually saw a decrease in Email Acquisition for All Traffic compared to the control, however this was not at a valid level of confidence. More testing is required.
Some questions to consider are the ways in which this framing shifted the responsibility onto the reader. Although the attempt was to not focus on why the organization created the resource so much as the user's journey and the impact they want to make — it's possible this put too much pressure on the reader. What if they aren't ready to disciple or commit to "come alongside someone?" The original used these terms, but only in the sense of whether the readers themselves has ever had someone do this for them, not whether they want to do this for someone else.
Future experimentation should continue to test different approaches to framing the language and offer on the DDJ landing page to determine the most effective strategy for increasing sign-up rates. It is important to gather more data and achieve a higher statistical level of confidence in order to make valid conclusions.

experiments.title: How prioritizing body copy over a header image affects landing page sign-up
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prioritizing the body copy over the header image for users arriving on the DDJ landing page will achieve an increase in sign-up rate because headline and copy will better reinforce the value of why they should sign up than an image will. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/03/2023 - 08/11/2023
The landing page for The Navigators’ Digital Disciple Journal is focused on name acquisition. In an attempt to increase sign-up rate on the page, we created two different treatments against the control. The first utilizes a smaller header image, our hypothesis is that this would prioritize the copy more and communicate more value when the user first arrives on the page versus only seeing the image and some of the headline. We theorized that especially in mobile, this would show the greatest improvement. For the second treatment, we eliminated the header images completely. When the user arrives on the page, they are immediately met with the headline and value copy of the body text. We belive both of these edits, though slight, would make an impact on sign-up by better prioritizing what the user sees and reads when they land on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that prioritizing the body copy over the header image on the landing page of the Digital Disciple Journal resulted in a very small different. The increase was only 2.5% with a level of confidence 26%. What this tells us is that despite rearranging the elements of the page, this is unlikely the reason why users are not converting on the page. Instead, it prompts us to turn our focus towards the elements themselves rather than their order. Whether they see the body copy when they first arrive or not, what does the body copy actually say?
In future experiments, it would be valuable to further explore these questions. Additionally, it would be beneficial to investigate other factors that may influence sign-up rate, such as the placement and prominence of the sign-up form, the call-to-action wording, or the use of social proof and testimonials

experiments.title: How a sticky donate button affects donor conversion rates on main pages on a website
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional opportunity to give for site visitors  will achieve an increase in donations because they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language. because  they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/21/2023
The Navigators wanted to take advantage of the traffic generated through their resource and blog pages. As people are being equipped/informed, we wanted to run a test where people would see a sticky donate button on the side that would prompt people to give. The treatment would slide out when someone scrolled halfway through the page. If they click “yes” they would be taken to a donation page to make their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an additional opportunity for site visitors to donate can potentially lead to an increase in donations. The treatment version, which had a sticky donate button that prompted visitors to give and reminded them of how The Navigators help others, saw a 28.0% increase in donations compared to the control version.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 70.2%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%.
It would be beneficial to test different versions of the treatment to further optimize the effectiveness of the sticky donate button. By testing different variations and monitoring the impact on donations, the organization can continue to refine and improve their approach to increasing donations.
Overall, the key learning is that adding an additional opportunity for site visitors to donate can potentially have a positive impact on donations. However, further testing and optimization are needed to determine the most effective approach.

experiments.title: How using clarity and an offer in a email subscription form impacts email acquisition rate

rows.name: Do you want to grow in your discipleship?

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and incentives in the subscription for site visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate because prospects will know what they’re getting and are further incentivized by receiving something of value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/13/2023 - 05/01/2023
Across their site, the Navigators has a footer where they prompt people to sign up for their emails. In order to capture more emails, we added value proposition language to this section to add clarity in the first variant. We also wanted to test using an ebook as additional incentive to sign up in the second variant. The use of these two different variants would give us some indicators as to what motivates people to subscribe to the Navigators’ communications.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment showed us that people were more drawn to take action when given clear proposition language over an ebook incentive.

Treatment 1 resulted in a 27% increase in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 64% while treatment 2 resulted in a 5% decrease with a level of confidence of 15%. In order to get valid results, Navigators will ran this test again with a 50/50 split with the first treatment.

experiments.title: How more concise copy on a content offer donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing the story from the donation page  for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in the donor conversion rate  because we’ve streamlined the message to focus on The Navigators’ mission and purpose. Without the narrative, potential donors can swiftly grasp crucial information without distraction, reducing the risk of donor fatigue and ensuring a more prominent and compelling call to action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2024 - 05/06/2024
In an effort to increase donor conversion on a content offer funnel, we decided to run a copy test on the donation page.
The control donation page features a story about the founder of The Navigators. While the story is relevant to the organization, we felt that it added unnecessary friction to the page because it was irrelevant to the offer.
Our hypothesis was that by removing the story from the donation page will increase the donor conversion rate because we’ve streamlined the message to focus on The Navigators’ mission and purpose. Without the narrative, potential donors can swiftly grasp crucial information without distraction, reducing the risk of donor fatigue and ensuring a more prominent and compelling call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the founder story from the donation page led to a 34.2% decrease in transactions for all traffic, indicating that the control version with the story performed better. However, with a statistical confidence level of 85%, these results are not considered valid or reliable for decision-making.
While these results were not considered valid, they do suggest that The Navigators audience prefers receiving the backstory and the meaning of the organization even within content offer funnels which is opposite of what we initially hypothesized.
Due to the fact that these results were trending in a negative direction, we chose to end the test and keep the all traffic going to the control.
Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider the impact of storytelling and personal connections in donation pages, as these elements can sometimes resonate strongly with potential donors and influence their decision to contribute. Further testing could explore how to incorporate storytelling effectively while still maintaining a clear and compelling call to action.

experiments.title: How a recurring interrupter impacts recurring donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a smaller, recurring donation for one-time project givers will achieve an increase in recurring giving because asking for a smaller gift is appealing to the donor. . because it reduces the perceived financial burden, making it easier for donors to commit regularly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2024 - 05/07/2024
In an effort to increase recurring donors for The Navigators, we decided to run a recurring interrupter on their main donation page. This test ran for a week and a half split 50/50 among traffic.
The interrupter only showed for individuals who initiated a one-time project gift under $100. The interrupter then asked the individual to upgrade their giving to a $10 monthly gift.
Our hypothesis was that asking for a gift smaller than what was originally initiated would lead to an increase in recurring giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the interrupter is converting some donors to monthly giving. Based on the results above, 11 people clicked "YES! Convert my gift to a $10 monthly gift." out of the 192 people who were shown the interrupter. This means that the interrupter converted 5.73% of people who saw it.
While we can't validate these results, it's clear that it is effective for at least 5% of individuals who see it. We also saw a 7.8% lift in overall recurring donations at a 35% LOC.
In future experimentation, we plan to extend the threshold of individuals who qualify to see the interrupter. Since the current cutoff is $100, we plan to extend this up to $500 with tiered levels of recurring asks accordingly and aim to get a valid lift in recurring donations.
Overall, it is encouraging to see the interrupter have a positive impact on gifts, and we hope to continually optimize this experience through testing.

experiments.title: How including a gift handle with the corresponding impact described in a fundraising email affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including gift handles with the corresponding impact described for potential donors to Eagle Lake will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because we’re providing clear, tangible examples of how different donation amounts will be used .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/22/2024
The Navigators are running their bi-annual Eagle Lake Campaign to raise money for this kids’ camp that operates every summer. In the kickoff email of this campaign, they wanted to test how including gift handles in the email would affect donor conversion rate. Each gift amount described what that amount would provide for Eagle Lake.
This experiment idea came from their direct mail piece which included “tickets” for people to purchase. Each ticket was for a different amount (same as the email) and described what each level of ticket would provide for the kids at Eagle Lake.
To keep the language consistent between the email and the donation page, the treatment donation page also had the gift handles in the body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including gift handles with corresponding impact descriptions in a fundraising email may not always lead to an increase in donor conversion rates. In this case, the treatment actually resulted in a decrease of 15.1% in donations for all traffic. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid or conclusive.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different strategies and elements in fundraising emails to see what resonates best with donors. This could include testing different gift handle amounts, impact descriptions, or even the placement of this information within the email. It's also important to ensure that experiments are conducted with a sufficient sample size to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence in the results.

Overall, the key takeaway is to not solely rely on the results of one experiment, especially if the statistical confidence level is below the recommended threshold. By continuing to test and iterate on different strategies, we can gather more reliable data to inform decision-making for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How using continuity in the headline of a donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a continuity headline for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it will provide more clarity on the next steps for the potential donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/27/2024 - 03/14/2024
The Navigators ran an acquisition offer this spring offering a set of Easter cards. In an effort to increase the donor conversion rate at the end of the funnel, we wanted to text additional continuity between the landing page to the donation page. The idea behind the test is that our donation pages may be perceived as confirmation pages when a visitor is working their way through a funnel. By adding a “continuation headline” we are able to better communicate the next steps to the visitor, therefore leading to the potential increase in donor conversion rate. We have seen success with other clients using this technique, so we wanted to see how The Navigators audience reacted to it. Our hypothesis was that the additional continuity would increase donor conversion rates.
Control:
Headline: Help us bring the disciple-makers to The Great Commission
Treatment:
Headline: Your Easter cards will be sent to you soon. Before you go, would you consider something?
Subheadline: Will you help us bring the disciple-makers to The Great Commission?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including a continutation headline for potential donors can potentially lead to an increase in donor conversion rates. In this specific case, the treatment group saw a 14.0% increase in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence of 44.5% is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%, making the results not statistically significant and not suitable for future decision-making.
However, due to the slight increase, it would be important to retest this concept for The Navigators. It is important to note that we were unable to let this test continue to run and potentially validate due to a lack of inventory of the offer. Therefore, we may be able to get valid results on a test that can run for a longer period or on a more evergreen offer like an ebook.
While transactions did not validate for this test, it is encouraging to see a relative lift that may indicate their audience responds well to a continuation headline.

experiments.title: How do the perceived benefits of a premium affect recurring donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that explaining the benefits of a premium book for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversions because the additional clarity on the premium will motivate them to click the link and learn more.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
For The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to test calling out the perceived benefits of the premium in an effort to pinpoint motivating factors for their audience. The control email called out the impact that their monthly gift would have on the organization whereas the treatment called out the benefits of reading the book that they would receive as a premium for becoming a monthly donor.
This test ran as a 50/50 split to their housefile audience in an effort to acquire more recurring donors as well as test what motivates their audience to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that emphasizing the benefits of a premium in a fundraising appeal may not necessarily lead to an increase in donor conversions. In this case, the treatment that focused on the benefits of receiving a book as a premium for becoming a monthly donor actually resulted in a decrease in donations compared to the control, which highlighted the impact of the monthly gift on the organization. The treatment saw a drop of 44.1% in transactions at a 67.7% level of confidence, which is below the validity threshold.

This copy test saw a valid increase in clicks, but an invalid drop in transactions. Therefore, indicating that the copy was persuasive in getting potential donors to click the link, but their audience was less motivated to give.

In future experimentation, it would be important to continue testing different messaging strategies to understand what truly motivates the audience to give. This could involve testing different types of premiums, highlighting various impacts of donations, or even testing different audience segments with tailored messaging. By continuously testing and learning from these experiments, we can gain a deeper understanding of our audience and optimize our fundraising strategies for better results.

experiments.title: How does receiving a premium compared to receiving personal recognition for their gift affect clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personal recognition for monthly partners will achieve an increase in clicks and donor conversion because donors would feel more valued by the organization and the personal touch of their name being added to the group.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
For The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to test whether a premium or personal recognition for their recurring gift would be more effective.
The control focused on the same premium that had been used throughout the campaign, and the treatment focused on a more personal recognition for their monthly gift. The treatment also promised to send the monthly partner a picture of their name on the window with all of the other monthly partners.
We hypothesized that the treatment would greatly outperform the control due to the personal touch and recognition of becoming a monthly partner because recognition is a motivating factor for the majority of people.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which focused on personal recognition for monthly partners, did not outperform the control, which offered a premium for recurring gifts. The treatment actually saw a drop of 3.7% in clicks at a 21.6% level of confidence. These results are inconclusive; however, we also wanted to look into if this test impact donations rather than clicks.

The control had a total of 13 donations, and the treatment had 8 donations. The results in terms of transactions show a drop of 36.1% in transactions at a 71.3% level of confidence. While transactions also did not validate, it indicates that The Navigators audience did not respond well to recognition for their gift.

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to further test the impact of personal recognition versus premiums for recurring donors, ensuring that the sample size is sufficient to draw valid conclusions. Additionally, considering other variables or elements that could influence donor behavior, such as messaging or incentives, could provide a more comprehensive understanding of what motivates donors to contribute.

experiments.title: How do the perceived benefits associated with becoming a recurring donor impact click-through rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the benefits of becoming a monthly partner  for the donor will achieve an increase in click through rate because  of the perceived exclusivity and incentives associated with becoming a monthly partner.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/27/2024
For The Navigator’s February Sustainers Appeal, we hypothesized that calling out the benefits of becoming a monthly partner would increase our click-through rate as well as our donor conversion rate. The control email emphasized the importance and need for monthly partners, focusing heavily on the difference it would make for the organization. Whereas, the treatment focused on the benefits for the donor themselves.
This test ran as a 50/50 split to their housefile audience in an effort to acquire more recurring donors as well as test what motivates their audience to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted for The Navigator's February Sustainers Appeal tested the impact of emphasizing the benefits of becoming a monthly partner for the donor, as opposed to focusing on the organization's need for monthly partners.
The treatment email, which highlighted the benefits for the donor, showed a 7.4% increase in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control email. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 52.7%, meaning the results cannot be considered statistically valid.
However, the treatment greatly outperformed the control in terms of transactions. The treatment saw a 161.3% lift in transactions at a 99.6% level of confidence, meaning that the results are valid!
Therefore, the key learnings from this experiment are that The Navigators housefile is much more motivated to give when exclusivity and incentives are coupled with an appeal. While the results showed that readers were slightly more inclined to click the link when benefits to the donor were included, the individuals who did take the next step within the treatment, were much more motivated to give.

experiments.title: How does a humanized sticky bar on a donation page impact conversion rate for a direct appeal?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar of the email sender for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the continuity from the email to the donation page will increase the personal connection and credibility of the sender.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2024 - 02/11/2024
For The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal, we ran a 50/50 split in traffic to the donation page, half of which would see the normal donation page, the other half would see a humanized sticky bar featuring the the email sender on the donation page.
Our hypothesis was that individuals who saw the humanized sticky bar of Scott’s face, the email sender, would be more inclined to give a gift because it creates a more personal experience and increases the credibility of the sender.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a humanized sticky bar of the email sender for potential donors may not always result in an increase in donor conversion rate. In this case, the treatment with the sticky bar actually saw a decrease in transactions of 14.9% at a 38.2% level of confidence, compared to the control group as well as a slight decrease in revenue at 0.13% with a 46.3% level of confidence.

Moving forward, it is important to design experiments with a larger sample size and longer duration to ensure more reliable results. Additionally, it is important to consider other factors that may have influenced the outcomes, such as the timing of the appeal, the messaging, or the overall design of the donation page. It is also important to note that because there was no CTA on this sticky bar our results may be invalid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be wise to continue testing the impact of humanized elements in donation appeals, but also to explore different variables and combinations to determine what resonates best with donors. We could potentially try a different email sender to see if a different person from the organization resonates with The Navigators audience more effectively.

experiments.title: How calling out security reinforcement impacts clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that calling out the security of a gift for potential donors will achieve an increase in the donor conversion rate because it will reduce the anxiety of making a gift by emphasizing that their personal information will remain confidential.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/19/2024
For The Navigator’s February Sustainers Appeal, we hypothesized that calling out the security of a gift within the email would increase the click-through rate as well as the donor conversion rate. This ran as a 50/50 split test to their housefile to test the effectiveness of security language for their audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment email resulted in a 9.2% drop in clicks at a 60.2% level of confidence as well as a 72.6% drop in donor conversion rate with an 80.2% level of confidence indicating that the control email was more effective. Since both of these metrics fall below the validation threshold of 95%, we can not conclude that The Navigators audience does not respond well to calling out the security of a gift, and it may be worth testing again. It is possible that calling out the security of the gift before the potential donor landed on the donation page caused additional confusion and friction. Furthermore, this campaign sent people to a recurring only donation form which could have played a role in the lack of validation of the experiment.

In future experimentation, it would be interesting to test the impact of a security callout on the donation page as it may convert more donors as well as test this concept on an appeal where one-time donations are also accepted.

experiments.title: How using an image of the content offer in a housefile email affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image to the email for potential downloaders will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because users can see the value of the offer at the beginning of the funnel.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/23/2024
The Navigators sent a free content offer to their housefile list through email. The content offer was a free 12-month calendar and users had to provide their name, email, and home address on the landing page to receive a physical copy. Following the landing page, they were redirected to an instant donation page where they were asked to make a gift to support the Navigators work.
Within the email, they wanted to test how adding an image of the calendar would affect click-through rate from the email to the landing page. They also wanted to know if adding the image to the email would affect donor conversion rate since users can see the value of the offer at the beginning of the funnel.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image to the email did not result in a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the threshold of 85%, which means these results should not be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it would be important to conduct a larger sample size or make adjustments to the experiment design to increase the statistical power and ensure valid results. The Navigators should also consider testing other variables or elements of the email and landing page to identify potential factors that may influence the donor conversion rate. It is important to continue conducting experiments and collecting data to make informed decisions and optimize the effectiveness of email campaigns in the future.

experiments.title: How positioning the donor as the hero impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing clarity on the donation page for donors and positioning them as the hero will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because they will have a clear understanding as to what their gift will do and how they will make an impact.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2023 - 11/30/2023
During The Navigator’s Giving Tuesday fundraising campaign, we wan a 50/50 split A/B test on the donation page. This copy test aimed to frame the donor as the hero to see how it impact the donor conversion rate. Our hypothesis was that making the impact of donation clear in the donation page copy would resonate more with potential donors, therefore, influencing them to complete their donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that providing clarity on the donation page and positioning the donor as the hero can potentially increase donor conversion rate. However, the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, with a confidence level of only 32.2%. Therefore, these results should not be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a higher level of statistical confidence is achieved before drawing conclusions or making decisions based on the results. This can be done by increasing the sample size or conducting more iterations of the experiment.
Overall, while the results of this experiment are inconclusive, it does provide some indication that providing clarity and positioning the donor as the hero may be effective strategies. These strategies can be further tested and refined in future experiments to determine their impact on donor conversion rate.

experiments.title: How does increasing the relevancy of a subject line affect email opens
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the relevancy of a subject line for cultivation email recipients will achieve an increase in email engagement because the timeliness of the message is increased.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/12/2023
The Navigators sent a cultivation email as apart of their calendar year end campaign that offered a Christmas Reading Plan for free to their housefile. The original subject line included exclusivity for the user as it said “A Christmas Reading Plan Just for You!” While we’ve seen exclusivity in copy perform well, we wanted to test how changing the subject line to be more timely to the message would affect email open rate. The new subject line read ” A must-read before Christmas!”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Changing the subject line to be more timely and relevant to the message can lead to an increase in email engagement.

2. But while the treatment showed a slight increase in opens, the statistical level of confidence was below the threshold of 85%, indicating that the results are not valid.

To improve future experimentation, testing different variations of subject lines and analyzing the impact on email engagement can provide further insights. It is also crucial to track other engagement metrics such as click-through rate and conversion rate to get a holistic view of the success of email campaigns.

experiments.title: How a sticky donate button affects donor conversion rates on main pages on a website
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional opportunity to give for site visitors will achieve an increase in donations because they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language. because they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/21/2023
The Navigators wanted to take advantage of the traffic generated through their resource and blog pages. As people are being equipped/informed, we wanted to run a test where people would see a sticky donate button on the side that would prompt people to give. The treatment would slide out when someone scrolled halfway through the page. If they click “yes” they would be taken to a donation page to make their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an additional opportunity for site visitors to donate can potentially lead to an increase in donations. The treatment version, which had a sticky donate button that prompted visitors to give and reminded them of how The Navigators help others, saw a 28.0% increase in donations compared to the control version.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 70.2%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%.

It would be beneficial to test different versions of the treatment to further optimize the effectiveness of the sticky donate button. By testing different variations and monitoring the impact on donations, the organization can continue to refine and improve their approach to increasing donations.

Overall, the key learning is that adding an additional opportunity for site visitors to donate can potentially have a positive impact on donations. However, further testing and optimization are needed to determine the most effective approach.

experiments.title: How using an eBook cover image on a landing page impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an eBook cover image for a free content offer will achieve an increase in email acquisition because there is additional clarity enhancing the perceived value of the offer on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/22/2023 - 12/21/2023
When sharing a digital magazine issue, we wondered if adding clarity to the landing page by showing a preview of the magazine cover would motivate more people to convert on the email acquisition page. We developed a treatment that included the eBook image alongside the copy and split traffic 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an eBook cover image to a landing page for a free content offer did not result in an increase in email acquisition. In fact, the treatment saw a decrease in email acquisition compared to the control group. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests with a larger sample size and longer duration to obtain more reliable results. Additionally, it may be worth exploring other factors that could potentially increase email acquisition, such as different call-to-action copy or alternative content offers. Continuous testing and experimentation will help to identify what strategies and elements are most effective in attracting and converting users.

experiments.title: How using a more concise donation page in a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and being more concise for potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because repeated information on a donation page is added friction to the donation process which can deter people from giving.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/21/2023
This ministry organization ran a high-urgency campaign to their full housefile with the goal of acquiring more recurring donors. The original donation page re-explained most points that the emails had already described, so we asked ourselves the questions:
Are subscribers still needing this much information by the time they get to the donation page? Or are they just ready to give, and we are getting in their way by proposing more hypothetical questions?
That’s why we proposed a second, short, to-the-point donation page that focuses on clarity and is more concise.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that a more concise and clear donation page may lead to a higher donor conversion rate. The treatment, which focused on eliminating repeated information and reducing friction in the donation process, saw a 0.9% increase in donations. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 4.8%, which is below the recommended 85%.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue exploring the impact of clarity and conciseness on donor conversion rates. The results of this experiment suggest that potential donors may prefer a streamlined and simplified donation process without excessive information repetition. Further testing with larger sample sizes and higher statistical power could help validate these findings and provide more reliable insights for decision making.
Additionally, it would also be valuable to explore other potential factors that could impact donor conversion rates, such as the design and layout of the donation page, messaging and storytelling techniques, and ease of use. By combining multiple variables and testing different combinations, a deeper understanding of the most effective strategies for acquiring recurring donors can be gained.

experiments.title: How a Radical Redesign Impacted Staff Donation Pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that re-positioning the donation page and better highlighting one-time vs monthly gifts for staff donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because moving the gift array to the bottom allows the user to read the copy and see the value first before being given an opportunity to give. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2023 - 04/06/2023
The Navigators’ saw tremendous success with a radical redesign of their instant donation pages that implemented a tabbed form and moved the gift array to the bottom of the page instead of the top. We hypothesized that creating the same treatment for Staff Donation Pages would result in a similar lift to conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, we saw neither a valid increase nor a decrease in this experiment. The relative difference in conversion rate was only 2.8% with a low level of confidence at 27.7%. A learning here is that simply because a treatment worked on one page doesn't mean that it will generate the same results on another. The minimal difference also urges consideration on how this treatment was being applied on a very different page to an audience with potentially very difference motivations than in the original test. For Staff Donation Pages, users are more likely to already know in advance who they'd want to support. It's possible that because they are coming to the page with an intentional motivation of who they are or are not giving to, this treatment did very little to affect this audience's decision.

experiments.title: How a Monthly Callout in the Gift Array Impacts Recurring Giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting the monthly giving option for users on the donation page will achieve an increase in recurring gifts because the copy reinforces the value of a monthly gift to the reader and drives home what a monthly gift accomplishes..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2023 - 05/14/2023
The Sustainers appeal is The Navigators recurring giving campaign. With users arriving on the donation page from a variety of emails, ads, and onsite assets, we hypothesized that by calling out the monthly gift option on the donation page itself would help increase recurring gifts. We believed that a simple value prop callout reminding the user of need for monthly gifts specifically would help convert those who would have originally given a one-time gift or neither at all.
experiments.keyLearnings: The A/B experiment conducted for The Navigators' Sustainers appeal showed that highlighting the monthly giving option on the donation page did not result in an increase in recurring gifts with validity. More experimentation is needed, it also prompts questions about more effective ways to test this like having the value prop callout more prominently in the copy itself or a headline.

experiments.title: How adding clarity and specificity to an email subscription form impacts email acquisition rate

rows.name: Do you want to grow in your discipleship?

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and specificity  for site visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate because there will be reduced anxiety when the provide their contact information .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/28/2023 - 06/02/2023
Across their site, the Navigators has a footer where they prompt people to sign up for their emails. In order to capture more emails, we added value proposition language to this section. The treatment copy let’s people know why they would want to be part of their email list and what specific communications they would be receiving. Our hypothesis is that this added clarity, specificity and value language would increase email acquisition rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment copy added to the footer section did not increase the email acquisition rate for the Navigators website. There was a 21% decrease with a level of confidence of 73%.
However, when looking at new vs returning users we saw the language resonate with returning users and new users saw a valid 41% decrease in email acquisition rate with a level of confidence of 95%. Our hypothesis is that the header language might be too high of a commitment for new users. We want to retest this concept and change the copy so that it's a lower barrier to entry for new users on their site.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the impact of a donor’s gift before a call to action, affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that treating the pushdown headline to first answer “why” for users on the homepage will achieve a higher conversion rate because leading with “what” a donation accomplishes for the user and “why” will be more compelling than the command in the control.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2023 - 05/14/2023
The Navigators created a pushdown/sticky bar for their Eagle Lake campaign. This asset targeted the main homepage, and the button would take users to the instant donation page for the campaign. At launch, the pushdown was underperforming compared to its effectiveness on past campaigns—we hypothesized that this was due to copy itself lacking value prop. In the control, the main headline starts with a call to give even before the user has been given any reasons as to why.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to improve the underperforming pushdown/sticky bar for The Navigators' Eagle Lake campaign by changing the copy of the headline to address the "why" of giving. The treatment resulted in a directional 13.9% increase in Revenue for All Traffic, but the statistical level of confidence was below the 85% threshold for validity.

Though not a valid result, we did see that the treatment captured

Ultimately, Navigator's audience seemed to respond similarly to both control and treatment versions of the copy. Of course, the next steps could be testing into new copy treatments but the question is whether this is the most likely angle to move the needle for Navigators' audience given the subdued reaction here. Instead, we'd like to turn our focus away from testing into copy and instead determine whether a visual change, like a goal thermometer, is what will prove most effective in communicating value.

experiments.title: How reducing the giving process from 2 pages to 1 page impacts donor conversion rate (pt. 2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the giving experience to one step  for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate  because reducing steps to a process can reduce friction in the giving process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/09/2023
In a previous experiment, the Navigators successfully increased donor conversion rate by reducing the number of steps in the giving process from 3 to 2. Our hypothesis was that we would reduce even more friction in the giving process by consolidating the experience into a single page. Unfortunately, the organization’s current giving platform was not able to accommodate a single step giving experience. Therefore, we used a completely different platform for this experiment’s treatment. We kept the control as-is in its current platform and the treatment on a new platform as a single-page experience.
In a previous iteration of this experiment we saw a valid 55% decrease in donor conversion rate. Our hypothesis was that the user may have experienced a break in branding congruity. So, we rebuilt this experiment on another offer and made the donation page look more like the original donation page in terms of colors and aesthetics.
We only ran this experiment in one ebook offer funnel and most of the traffic was driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment resulted in a 24.3% decrease in donations for all traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 73%, indicating that the results are not valid.
Though slightly better in results from our previous experiment, we're still seeing a decrease in donor conversion rate which was not expected.
Additionally, we thought that the control might be more successful in converting returning donors because there is a "log-in" feature in the original platform that may be a convenient way for pre-existing donors to give. However, when looking at new vs. returning donors in each segment, there is virtually no impact in conversion rate among each segment.
Ultimately, we believe that there are too many new variables introduced in a completely new platform that could've impacted giving. It may not be that reducing the experience down to 1 step is reducing conversion rate. Other features like not being able to personalize the first name, or including Anedot express giving/Google pay, are features we did not initially account for.

experiments.title: How adding an additional ask in the P.S. line of an appeal email impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an ask in the P.S. line of an email for email recipients will achieve more donors and revenue .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/04/2022
The Navigators were running a short email campaign for their collegiate ministries. In order to increase donor conversion rate and revenue we wanted to see how adding one additional opportunity to give would impact giving. Typically, The Navigators’ have used the “P.S” line as a place to opt out of communications from that specific campaign. We wanted to test using the P.S. line as an additional ask with a link to the donation page. The control for this experiment used the P.S. line to opt out and the treatment used the P.S. line for a call to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When looking at the donor conversion rate, there was virtually no difference. We saw a .2% lift in donor conversion rate with only a level of confidence of .4%--making it a statistically insignificant difference. We did however see a 74% directional lift in revenue with a level of confidence of 86.9%. While this seems positive, it is unlikely that an additional ask would sway a prospective donor to give significantly more than they were originally intending. Furthermore, we only had 29 transactions in this experiment and did not reach the ideal 50 transaction minimum. While there are no outlier gifts, it is more likely that the list was split in such a way that giving level wasn't evenly stratified and the treatment may have had larger donors or more key multiyear donors.

It is recommended that this is retested with an evenly stratified list across multiple emails to gain a more concrete learning.

experiments.title: How reducing the giving experience from 3 steps to 2 steps increases donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the steps to donate for ebook downloaders will achieve a high donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/12/2022 - 06/27/2022
Since changing giving platforms, the Navigators giving experience is now a 3 step process after requesting an ebook on a landing page. After a site visitor fills out the form to get an offer, they’re taken to a landing page with the value prop language and button on the bottom to give. When people click on the button they’re taken to a generic giving page with a gift array. They are then given the option to “add to basket” and are taken to a page to fill out their credit card information. Our hypothesis is that if we can reduce this experience down to 2 steps, site visitors will experience less friction and we’ll see a higher donor conversion rate.
To test this concept we ran this experiment through a specific funnel/offer: “Fight on my knees” ebook. Traffic to this funnel is mainly driven by paid Facebook ads.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a 65% lift in donor conversion rate but we only saw a level of confidence of 53.9% which means this experiment did not validate. Even after running for over a month, this funnel did not get enough traffic in order to acquire a large enough sample size. It is recommended that this either retested in a different funnel that has more traffic or we test using a 1 page donation process if at all possible.

experiments.title: How longer, value prop-based language impacts donor conversion rate in a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that longer, value-prop focused language on a donation page for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/24/2022 - 06/25/2022
The Navigators were running a high urgency campaign for their Missionary Care fund. In order to increase donor conversion rate, we wanted to test longer, value prop-focused language on the donation page. Our hypothesis is that the longer copy will drive more donations because it is giving the prospective donor a fuller reason to give towards Missionary Care. Traffic to this donation page came from campaign emails and sticky bar traffic from their website.
experiments.keyLearnings: While initially the results seemed to indicate that the longer copy outperformed the shorter, direct copy, the experiment ultimately resulted in a 17% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 57%. The treatment made no substantial difference to the end results. This concept can be retested by moving the gift array to the bottom oof the donation page so that the site visitor needs to read through the copy before getting to the intended call to action. It's likely that because the gift array was at the top, some site visitors may not have scrolled down to read the intended copy.

experiments.title: How removing an image and adding a reader-centric headline affect email acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing an image and providing a reader-centric headline for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email acquisition rate..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2022 - 06/23/2022
The Navigators were running acquisition ads to promote their 7 Tips to Help Others Grow in Christ offer. As they tried to optimize ad creative, they also looked at the landing page to see if there was anything that might be hindering conversion.
They hypothesized that the size of the image might be pushing the form and value proposition too far down the page (especially on mobile), and that removing the image might increase conversion. At the same time, they also noticed that the headline simply declared the title of the offer, and didn’t offer any value to the reader. They decided to test this as well in a “C” variant, which compounded on top of the image removal.
They split the traffic three ways to determine the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, neither experiment had a material impact on conversion, regardless of device type. This showed them that the image was not hindering conversion (and removal of it didn't help), and that the new headline did not increase motivation or clarity to accept the offer.
They wanted to try a radical redesign next that dramatically altered the layout of the page to see if that would move the needle.

experiments.title: How aligning the image and value proposition for a content offer affect email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the image with the offer and increasing the clarity of the value proposition for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2021 - 06/12/2021
The Navigators were promoting their Digital Discipleship Journey resource through Facebook ads. Their cost-per-name was above what they projected, and they wanted to see how they could optimize their results. A previous test had shown that an image that was more specific to the offer greatly increased conversion rate and CPA, so they wanted to apply that to the landing page as well. Additionally, they wanted to test the content on the landing page to see if increasing clarity of the offer and removing vertical space could increase conversion.
They created two treatments and ran an A/B/C test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Ultimately, neither landing page made a significant difference in conversion rate. There was directional evidence that the new image provided a slight bump to conversion rate, but it wasn't near significance.
The 29% conversion rate on the landing page is still low compared to other Navigators offers, but this test suggests that the issue is upstream from the landing page—perhaps in the targeting or in the advertising.

experiments.title: How visual reinforcement of a premium affects donor conversion for a postal chase donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that visual reinforcement of a premium for postal chase visitors will achieve an increase in donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2021 - 05/22/2021
The Navigators were sending Easter cards as a premium offer to their direct mail file during the Easter season. They created a donation page for this campaign to receive traffic from the direct mail campaign and also as a landing point for Facebook ads which were shown to that audience to drive additional giving.
The donation page was very short and to the point. On reviewing it, the team wondered if adding a picture of the Easter cards (to reinforce what was sent in the mail) would increase conversion. They put an image of the card on the page, and added an additional paragraph of incentive-focused copy to remind them of the free premium they received.
They split the traffic to see which treatment converted more donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While this experiment didn't validate due to low overall traffic, it reveals more tactics that can be used to directionally increase conversion on these pages.
While the same audience is targeted with both campaigns, we must remember that there is a time-delay between the direct mail piece arriving and seeing the digital ad, and must overcompensate to remind them of the piece they received in the mail (which could be one of many).
